steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while the device is in active patient use. Transfer the patient to another functioning monitor before removing power or components.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is plugged into a known working hospital-grade outlet. Test the outlet with another device if needed. Confirm wall switch (if present) is ON.

steps.title: Check Power Cord and Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect the AC power cord for damage, bent prongs, or loose connections. Reseat the cord at both the wall and monitor. Try a known-good power cord.

steps.title: Confirm Power Indicator Lights
steps.instructions: Observe the monitor’s power/charging indicators. Look for charging LED or battery icon behavior. No indicator may suggest no AC input or internal PSU issue.

steps.title: Check Docking Station or Mount (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Ensure it is fully seated in the dock. Inspect dock contacts for dirt, bent pins, or damage. Try removing from dock and plugging directly into AC.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the battery (if accessible). Check for swelling, leakage, or damage. Verify battery age if known.

steps.title: Perform a Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Disconnect AC power. Remove battery (if possible). Wait 30–60 seconds. Reconnect and power on.

steps.title: Test with a Known-Good Battery
steps.instructions: If available, install a known-good battery. If the new battery charges → original battery is faulty. If not → likely charging circuit or power supply issue.

steps.title: Check for UPS / Internal Power Alarms
steps.instructions: Review any alarms related to internal battery failure. Review UPS/power supply fault messages.

steps.title: Verify Operation on AC Only
steps.instructions: Remove the battery and attempt to run on AC power alone. If unit does not power on → possible internal power supply failure. If it runs normally → issue likely isolated to battery system.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor not charging battery and shuts down when unplugged from AC power.
CCR.Cause: Battery failed to accept charge and did not hold power; verified with known-good battery test.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced defective battery and confirmed proper charging and operation on battery power.

helpfulDetails: Power cord condition and swap performed
Indicator lights or charging icon behavior

title: Drager Infinity Delta Series - Network or central station connectivity loss
description: Guide for troubleshooting network or central station connectivity issues on the Drager Infinity Delta Series patient monitor.

steps.title: Verify the Monitor is Powered On and Fully Booted
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is on and not in a startup or error state. A partially booted system may not establish network communication.

steps.title: Check Network Cable Connection (Hardwired Systems)
steps.instructions: Confirm the Ethernet cable is securely connected at the monitor and wall port. Look for link/activity lights on the port (if visible). Reseat the cable to rule out a loose connection.

steps.title: Inspect the Network Cable for Damage
steps.instructions: Check for bent pins, frayed cable, or damaged connectors. Swap with a known good cable to rule out cable failure.

steps.title: Verify Wall Port Functionality
steps.instructions: Test the wall port using another known working device if possible. If the port is not active, the issue may be upstream (switch/network).

steps.title: Confirm Correct Network Jack is Being Used
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is plugged into the designated patient monitoring network port, not a general data or inactive port.

steps.title: Check Network Settings on the Monitor
steps.instructions: Verify IP address, subnet, and gateway (if accessible). Ensure the monitor is configured for DHCP or static IP per facility standards. Incorrect settings can prevent communication with the central station.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor
steps.instructions: Power cycle the monitor to reset network communication. This can clear temporary communication or software faults.

steps.title: Check Central Station Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the central station is operational and not down. Verify other monitors are connecting successfully.

steps.title: Look for Network or Communication Alarms
steps.instructions: Note any error messages such as “network disconnected” or “server unavailable.” These can help identify whether the issue is local or system-wide.

steps.title: Verify Device is Assigned/Admitted Correctly
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is properly associated with a patient/location in the system. Misconfiguration may appear as a connectivity issue.

steps.title: Check for Recent Network Changes
steps.instructions: Determine if IT performed updates, VLAN changes, or switch maintenance. Network configuration changes commonly impact connectivity.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor not showing on central station; staff reports intermittent disconnects.
CCR.Cause: Loose Ethernet cable connection identified at monitor; no link light observed.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated and replaced Ethernet cable; verified network connection restored and monitor visible on central station.

title: Drager Infinity Delta Series - Display or Touchscreen/Keypad Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting unresponsive, partially responsive, or abnormal display, touchscreen, or keypad behavior on Drager Infinity Delta monitors.

steps.title: Check Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is powered on and battery has sufficient charge. Observe any low battery or power warning indicators. Why: Low power can cause unresponsive displays or input failures.

steps.title: Inspect Display and Touchscreen/Keypad for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Look for cracks, scratches, liquid spills, or debris. Ensure connectors are free of dust and moisture. Why: Physical damage or contamination can prevent proper touch/key response.

steps.title: Perform a Soft Reboot
steps.instructions: Power cycle the monitor using the standard shutdown and restart procedure. Observe if display and input responsiveness returns. Why: Temporary software glitches often cause touch/display failures.

steps.title: Check Display and Touchscreen/Keypad Connections
steps.instructions: Verify that internal cables (if accessible without disassembly) are seated properly in docking stations or external modules. For bedside monitors, ensure keyboard/touchscreen overlays are properly aligned. Why: Loose connections can cause partial or total input/display failures.

steps.title: Test with External Display or Input (if supported)
steps.instructions: If the monitor supports an external display or keyboard, connect and verify functionality. Why: Helps isolate whether the issue is with the touchscreen/keypad versus internal processing.

steps.title: Check Software/System Messages
steps.instructions: Review any error codes or on-screen messages. Confirm software version is current and not flagged for known display issues. Why: Some failures are software-related and may require an update.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor touchscreen and keypad not responding; screen frozen.
CCR.Cause: Power verified, cables seated, soft reboot attempted; display remains unresponsive.
CCR.Resolution: Monitor removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for manufacturer repair.

helpfulDetails: Any error messages or codes observed

title: Drager Infinity Delta Series - Alarm / Audio System Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting situations where the Infinity Delta monitor alarm system fails or produces inconsistent audio.

steps.title: Verify the Monitor is Powered On Properly
steps.instructions: Check that the monitor is plugged into a reliable power source or that the battery is sufficiently charged. Reason: Some audio functions are disabled or altered if power is low.

steps.title: Check the Volume Settings
steps.instructions: Access the monitor’s alarm settings menu. Confirm the volume is not muted or set to minimum. Reason: The most common “no sound” issue is a misconfigured volume.

steps.title: Ensure Alarm Limits Are Properly Set
steps.instructions: Verify that alarm thresholds are active and within the patient’s expected range. Reason: Some monitors suppress audio if no alarms are active or limits are outside the patient data range.

steps.title: Inspect External Connections
steps.instructions: If external speakers or headsets are used, ensure connections are secure and compatible. Reason: Loose cables or disconnected audio accessories can prevent alarm sound.

steps.title: Check for Alarm Silencing Features
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is not in a temporary silence, pause, or “mute” mode. Reason: Staff may have temporarily silenced the alarm, causing apparent failure.

steps.title: Observe Indicator Lights and Screen Prompts
steps.instructions: Look for any alert icons, system warnings, or error messages on the display. Reason: Some system warnings may disable alarm audio until resolved.

steps.title: Test Using a Known Alarm Condition
steps.instructions: Trigger a test alarm (for example, disconnect a sensor or use monitor’s built-in test mode if available). Reason: Confirms whether the issue is with audio hardware, settings, or actual alarm detection.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor alarm is not sounding; staff report missing audible alerts.
CCR.Cause: Alarm volume was found muted and external speaker cable loosely connected.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected speaker, restored volume settings, verified alarm sounds. Monitor returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Any system warnings or error codes noted
Final monitor status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Drager Infinity Kappa Series - MMS / Parameter Module Not Recognized or Communication Error
description: Guide for troubleshooting Infinity Kappa monitors when MMS or parameter modules are not recognized or show communication errors.

steps.title: Verify Patient Safety
steps.instructions:
Ensure the patient is on another functional monitor or appropriate monitoring device.
Do not troubleshoot on an actively monitored patient.

steps.title: Check Module Connection
steps.instructions:
Confirm the MMS/parameter module is properly seated in the monitor slot.
Remove and reseat the module to ensure firm connection.
Inspect for bent pins, debris, or visible damage.

steps.title: Inspect Cables and Connectors
steps.instructions:
Check all module cables and patient leads for wear, kinks, or loose connections.
Swap with a known working cable if available to rule out cable failure.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor
steps.instructions:
Safely power down the monitor.
Wait 10–15 seconds, then power it back on.
Observe if the monitor recognizes the module on reboot.

steps.title: Verify Module Compatibility and Configuration
steps.instructions:
Confirm the module is supported by the Infinity Kappa monitor.
Check the monitor’s software version; some modules require specific firmware.

steps.title: Check Alarm and Communication Settings
steps.instructions:
Review monitor menus for module recognition settings.
Ensure no “module off” or “parameter disabled” settings are active.

steps.title: Swap Modules (if available)
steps.instructions:
Replace with a known working module to see if the problem follows the module or the monitor.
If the replacement works, the original module may be faulty.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor does not recognize MMS/parameter module; parameters missing.
CCR.Cause: Module was not seated properly and initial cable inspection showed no damage.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated module and verified recognition; monitor functioning normally. If issue recurs, remove from service for repair.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior: Any intermittent or continuous alarms
Unusual sounds: Clicking or beeping from module
Final device status: Functioning / Out of Service

title: Drager Infinity Kappa Series - Battery Not Charging or Power / UPS Failure
description: Guide to troubleshoot battery charging and power/UPS issues on the Drager Infinity Kappa patient monitor.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is plugged into a live outlet or approved UPS. Check outlet with a known working device to confirm power availability. Why: No power input will prevent battery charging or operation.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Connections
steps.instructions: Examine the power cable for damage, fraying, or bent connectors. Confirm the connector is fully seated in the monitor’s power port. Why: Loose or damaged cables are common causes of charging failures.

steps.title: Check UPS or Surge Protector
steps.instructions: If using a UPS, ensure it is powered on and functioning. Bypass the UPS and connect directly to a known live outlet. Why: Faulty UPS can prevent proper charging or trigger power alarms.

steps.title: Verify Battery Status and Seating
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the battery according to manufacturer guidelines. Inspect battery contacts for corrosion, dirt, or damage. Why: Improperly seated or faulty batteries will not charge.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor
steps.instructions: Disconnect AC power, remove battery (if removable), wait 30 seconds, reconnect, and attempt power-on. Why: Resetting electronics can clear minor faults and restore charging recognition.

steps.title: Check for Error Messages or LED Indicators
steps.instructions: Note any battery, UPS, or system fault messages on the screen. Compare against the monitor manual for specific warnings. Why: Provides clues whether the issue is battery, UPS, or internal electronics.

CCR.Complaint: User reports the Infinity Kappa monitor battery will not charge and the unit shuts down when unplugged.
CCR.Cause: AC power and UPS verified; power cable intact; battery properly seated; monitor still fails to charge.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled “Out of Service – Battery/Power Fault,” and sent to Biomedical Service for repair.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested with a known device.
Error messages or LED indicators noted.
Final device status: Out of Service.

title: Drager Infinity Kappa Series - Network or Central Station Connectivity Loss
description: Guide for troubleshooting network connectivity or central station communication issues on the Infinity Kappa monitor.

steps.title: Check Monitor Network Status
steps.instructions: Verify the monitor’s network indicator lights. Confirm the Ethernet cable is connected and not damaged. Why: A loose or damaged cable is a common cause of connectivity loss.

steps.title: Confirm Network Port and Switch
steps.instructions: Ensure the wall outlet or switch port the monitor is connected to is active. Test with a known working device. Why: Network ports can be inactive due to maintenance, misconfiguration, or failure.

steps.title: Check IP and Network Settings
steps.instructions: Access the monitor’s network configuration. Confirm the IP address, subnet mask, and gateway match your network documentation. Why: Incorrect network settings can prevent communication with the central station.

steps.title: Restart Monitor and Central Station Connection
steps.instructions: Power cycle the monitor. If possible, reboot the central station interface handling the monitor. Why: Temporary software or network glitches can be cleared with a restart.

steps.title: Test Alternate Network Cable/Port
steps.instructions: Swap the monitor to a different port or replace the Ethernet cable. Why: Faulty cables or ports are frequent causes of intermittent connectivity.

steps.title: Check VLAN, Firewall, or Security Configurations
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor’s VLAN or network segment allows communication with the central station. Check with network/security team if any firewall rules may block traffic. Why: Network segmentation or security rules can prevent communication even if physical connections are correct.

steps.title: Observe Network Logs and Alarms
steps.instructions: Review monitor and central station logs for error messages or dropped packets. Why: Logs can provide insight into intermittent or systemic network issues.

CCR.Complaint: Infinity Kappa monitor not visible on central station; intermittent disconnects observed.
CCR.Cause: Ethernet cable was loose; network settings mismatched with central station VLAN.
CCR.Resolution: Re-seated Ethernet cable, corrected IP settings, verified connection on central station. Device fully functional.

helpfulDetails: Any observed intermittent connectivity or alarms
Final device status (operational / out of service)

title: Drager Infinity Kappa Series - Display or Touchscreen / Keypad Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting unresponsive or abnormal display, touchscreen, or keypad behavior on the Infinity Kappa monitor.

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is plugged into a working outlet or UPS. Check battery status if on battery operation. Why: A weak power supply can cause display or touch issues.

steps.title: Inspect Physical Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure all cables and connectors to the display module are secure. Check for visible damage, loose connectors, or bent pins. Why: Loose or damaged cables can prevent proper touchscreen or keypad function.

steps.title: Check for External Interference
steps.instructions: Remove any nearby strong electromagnetic devices. Ensure monitor isn’t exposed to liquids or extreme temperatures. Why: Environmental factors can temporarily affect touchscreen response.

steps.title: Perform a Soft Reboot
steps.instructions: Power cycle the monitor according to manufacturer guidelines. Observe if the display initializes normally. Why: Temporary software glitches can cause unresponsiveness.

steps.title: Inspect On-Screen Indicators and Error Messages
steps.instructions: Look for any error codes on startup or during operation. Record messages for reference. Why: Error codes can indicate module or software issues requiring escalation.

steps.title: Test Touchscreen and Keypad Inputs
steps.instructions: Attempt standard navigation and input across all areas of the screen and keypad. Note any unresponsive regions or inconsistent behavior. Why: Pinpoints whether the problem is localized (screen area) or generalized.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor touchscreen and keypad unresponsive; display flickers intermittently.
CCR.Cause: Power confirmed, cables secure, environmental factors normal, soft reboot ineffective; inputs fail across entire screen and keypad.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for bench evaluation/repair.

helpfulDetails: Any visible damage to cables or connectors.

title: Drager Infinity Kappa Series - Alarm / Audio System Failure or No Sound
description: Guide to troubleshoot the Infinity Kappa monitor alarm system when no sound or inconsistent audio is observed, focusing on external checks and safe escalation.

steps.title: Verify Monitor Power and Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is powered on and has no low battery or UPS alarms. Confirm the power cable is fully seated and that the monitor has stable AC or battery power. Reason: Insufficient power can affect the alarm circuitry or mute audio output.

steps.title: Check Audio Volume and Settings
steps.instructions: Access the monitor’s settings menu to verify that alarm volume is not muted or set too low. If the monitor has separate volume levels for different alarm priorities (high, medium, low), check all levels. Reason: Accidental changes in volume settings are a common cause of “no sound.”

steps.title: Inspect External Speakers or Cables (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Some Infinity Kappa monitors can be connected to external audio systems; verify connections. Swap cables or connectors if needed to rule out broken wires. Reason: External speaker failure or cable damage can mimic an internal alarm failure.

steps.title: Verify Alarm Configuration
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is configured to sound alarms for all active parameters. Check that alarms are not silenced globally or per parameter. Reason: Incorrect alarm configuration may appear as a failure of the audio system.

steps.title: Test With Another Monitor
steps.instructions: If possible, place the patient on a known-good monitor and check that alarms function properly. Reason: This isolates the issue to the monitor or environment rather than patient parameters.

steps.title: Observe Indicator Lights or Error Messages
steps.instructions: Look for any on-screen alerts or warning icons indicating audio system faults. Note any beeping patterns or LED indicators that might signify internal errors. Reason: Error codes may indicate the problem is beyond external checks.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor alarms are not producing sound; staff cannot hear alerts.
CCR.Cause: Alarm volume was verified; external connections and settings checked; no audible output detected.
CCR.Resolution: Monitor removed from service, labeled “Out of Service,” and sent for repair. Patient transferred to another functional monitor.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual display alerts or error messages noted
Final device status documented as out of service

title: Drager Primus - Pre-use Check or System Self-Test Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting Drager Primus ventilators that fail pre-use checks or system self-tests, focusing on external and easily verifiable causes.

steps.title: Ensure Safe Environment
steps.instructions: Confirm ventilator is not connected to a patient. Move patient to another ventilator if in use. Pre-use checks can fail if performed while patient circuits are attached.

steps.title: Verify Power Supply
steps.instructions: Check that the AC mains power is on and stable. Confirm battery is charged and connected properly. Voltage fluctuations or low battery can cause self-test failure.

steps.title: Check Connections and Circuit Setup
steps.instructions: Ensure all breathing circuit components are correctly attached. Inspect the CO2 sampling line, oxygen sensor, and airway pressure lines for disconnections, kinks, or leaks. Loose or misconnected sensors often cause pre-use check failure.

steps.title: Inspect Consumables and Filters
steps.instructions: Confirm that airway filters, HME filters, and expiratory valves are correctly installed. Remove or replace any disposable components if damaged or improperly seated.

steps.title: Examine Sensor Calibration
steps.instructions: Oxygen and pressure sensors should be calibrated according to manufacturer guidelines. Check for error codes related to the gas analyzer or flow sensors. Recalibration may be needed if external factors (temperature, humidity) have shifted readings.

steps.title: Run System Self-Test Again
steps.instructions: Power cycle the ventilator: turn off, wait 30 seconds, then turn on. Perform the pre-use check/self-test with all external connections properly in place.

CCR.Complaint: Primus ventilator displays ‘System Self-Test Failed’ when attempting pre-use check.
CCR.Cause: CO2 sampling line disconnected, preventing gas sensor from completing pre-use check.
CCR.Resolution: Reattached CO2 line and verified all connections; pre-use check passed.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual alarms, sounds, or heat

title: Drager Primus - Gas Supply or Agent Delivery Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting Drager Primus ventilators with gas supply or agent delivery errors, emphasizing external checks before internal failure assumptions.

steps.title: Verify Power and Startup
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is plugged into a verified medical-grade outlet and turned on. Confirm no startup errors unrelated to gas or agent delivery. Why: Proper startup ensures the device can correctly detect and manage gas and agent flows.

steps.title: Check Gas Supply Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect oxygen and air hoses for secure connections and absence of kinks or leaks. Confirm that gas supply pressures are within the recommended range (check wall supply gauges). Why: Low or absent gas supply is a common cause of delivery errors.

steps.title: Inspect Agent Vaporizers
steps.instructions: Ensure the vaporizer is correctly seated and locked. Verify the agent level is adequate and the correct agent type is installed. Check that the vaporizer is powered if it is electronically controlled. Why: Mis-seated or empty vaporizers often trigger agent delivery errors.

steps.title: Run Automated Self-Test / Leak Test
steps.instructions: Execute the ventilator’s system check or leak test per standard pre-use protocol. Observe for alarms related to gas flow or agent delivery. Why: The system check isolates whether errors are external (connections, supplies) or internal.

steps.title: Inspect Flow Sensors and Lines
steps.instructions: Confirm inspiratory and expiratory flow sensors are correctly installed and clean. Ensure no moisture, condensation, or blockages in the breathing circuit. Why: Obstructed flow paths can mimic gas delivery errors.

steps.title: Confirm Agent Control Settings
steps.instructions: Verify settings for concentration and delivery mode. Ensure alarms are configured correctly and no limits are preventing agent delivery.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator displays “gas supply error” and “agent delivery error” alarms during pre-use check.
CCR.Cause: Oxygen and air supply pressures within normal range, vaporizer correctly seated, flow sensors clean. Leak test completed with failure indicating internal gas module error.
CCR.Resolution: Ventilator removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent to manufacturer for bench repair.

helpfulDetails: Gas supply pressures and source verification.
Vaporizer type, agent level, and installation status.
Flow sensor status and circuit inspection notes.
Specific error codes displayed on the ventilator.
Outcome of self-test or leak test.

title: Drager Primus - Bellows Not Cycling Correctly
description: Troubleshooting guide for Drager Primus ventilators with bellows that fail to cycle correctly.

steps.title: Verify Power and Initialization
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator is plugged into a working outlet and the main power switch is on. Ensure battery backup is charged. Why: The bellows will not cycle if the unit is not powered correctly.

steps.title: Check Gas Supply Lines
steps.instructions: Verify that both oxygen and air sources are connected and turned on. Check for kinks, leaks, or loose fittings in the supply lines. Why: Insufficient gas flow prevents proper bellows inflation.

steps.title: Inspect the Bellows Assembly and Circuit
steps.instructions: Ensure the bellows is seated correctly in the ventilator. Confirm the breathing circuit is connected and free of obstructions. Look for moisture or condensation that may impede movement. Why: Improper placement or obstruction can block bellows movement.

steps.title: Review Ventilator Settings
steps.instructions: Check tidal volume, respiratory rate, and inspiratory/expiratory pressures. Ensure settings are within safe operational ranges. Why: Extreme settings can prevent the bellows from cycling normally.

steps.title: Check Pressure Relief Valves and Filters
steps.instructions: Inspect the exhalation valve and any inline filters for blockages. Replace if visibly obstructed or soiled. Why: Obstructed valves create resistance, preventing proper cycling.

steps.title: Perform a Pre-Use Self-Test
steps.instructions: Run the built-in system check. Observe whether errors are generated. Why: This can identify internal sensor or mechanical failures.

CCR.Complaint: User reports the bellows does not rise or fall properly during ventilation.
CCR.Cause: Observed that gas supply lines were connected but the exhalation valve had minor obstruction preventing normal bellows cycling.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared obstruction from exhalation valve and verified bellows cycled correctly during pre-use self-test. If unresolved, ventilator removed from service and sent for vendor evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exhalation valve and filters inspected: Yes
Error messages during self-test: None / Record if present
Final device status: Operational / Out of Service if unresolved

title: Drager Primus - Flow Sensor Calibration Fault
description: This guide assists Clinical Engineering in troubleshooting a Drager Primus ventilator displaying a flow sensor calibration fault, ensuring accurate tidal volume and airflow measurement.

steps.title: Verify Power and Startup
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is plugged into a verified working outlet. Power cycle the device. Why: Temporary electronic glitches can sometimes clear with a restart.

steps.title: Inspect Breathing Circuit Connections
steps.instructions: Confirm that all inspiratory and expiratory hoses are properly connected and seated. Check for leaks, kinks, or obstructions in the tubing. Why: Misconnected or obstructed tubing can falsely trigger flow sensor errors.

steps.title: Check Flow Sensor and Adapter
steps.instructions: Remove the flow sensor module and inspect for visible damage, moisture, or debris. Re-seat it firmly into its connector. Why: Poor seating or contamination can prevent proper calibration.

steps.title: Run a Device Self-Test
steps.instructions: Perform the ventilator’s pre-use check or system self-test. Observe if the fault persists. Why: Confirms whether the problem is persistent or intermittent.

steps.title: Inspect Gas Supply and Filters
steps.instructions: Ensure oxygen, air, and other gases are within acceptable pressure ranges. Check any inline filters for blockage or contamination. Why: Abnormal gas flow can interfere with flow sensor calibration.

steps.title: Swap Flow Sensor Module (if available)
steps.instructions: Replace the flow sensor with a known good module. Repeat self-test. Why: Confirms whether the fault is sensor-specific or system-wide.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator displays “Flow Sensor Calibration Fault” during pre-use check.
CCR.Cause: Flow sensor module either misaligned or failed calibration; initial inspection of connections and tubing showed no leaks or obstruction.
CCR.Resolution: Flow sensor module replaced with verified working module; device retested successfully. If fault persisted, ventilator sent to service bench.

helpfulDetails: Sensor module condition noted (clean, dry, undamaged)

title: Drager Primus - Touchscreen or Display Errors
description: This guide assists Clinical Engineering in troubleshooting Drager Primus ventilators that exhibit touchscreen or display errors.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Never attempt touchscreen troubleshooting on an actively ventilated patient. Move the patient to another ventilator before testing or powering off the device.

steps.title: Check power connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is plugged into a verified working outlet. Verify the power cable is fully seated on both ends. Why: Inconsistent or low voltage can cause display malfunctions.

steps.title: Verify battery status
steps.instructions: If operating on battery, confirm sufficient charge. Plug into AC power to see if the issue persists. Why: Low battery can cause display dimming or unresponsiveness.

steps.title: Inspect the touchscreen surface
steps.instructions: Clean with a soft, lint-free cloth and approved disinfectant. Remove any moisture or debris that may interfere with touch input. Why: Dirt, liquid, or residue can cause false or no touch responses.

steps.title: Check for visible damage
steps.instructions: Examine the screen for cracks, chips, or signs of impact. Inspect connector areas at the display module if accessible. Why: Physical damage can prevent proper display operation.

steps.title: Perform a soft reboot
steps.instructions: Power cycle the ventilator: turn off, wait 30 seconds, turn on. Observe if the display initializes correctly. Why: Temporary software glitches can be cleared with a reboot.

steps.title: Check ventilator firmware and settings
steps.instructions: Confirm the device firmware is current if information is available. Reset display settings to default if user-accessible. Why: Corrupt settings or outdated firmware may cause display errors.

steps.title: Swap external peripherals
steps.instructions: Disconnect any external modules, sensors, or data interfaces. Test if the display functions normally with minimal setup. Why: Peripheral conflicts can sometimes freeze or corrupt the touchscreen interface.

CCR.Complaint: Touchscreen unresponsive and display shows flickering or blank screen.
CCR.Cause: Observed that the ventilator operates normally on power cycle with no peripheral connections, but display fails intermittently. Cleaning and soft reboot did not correct the issue.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from clinical service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to biomedical engineering bench for display module evaluation or replacement.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Out of Service

title: Drager Primus IE Anesthesia Machine - System Self-Test or Pre-Use Check Failure
description: Identifies causes for failed system self-tests or pre-use checks, including power, sensor, connection, or environmental issues before clinical use.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is connected to the device. Move the patient to an alternate anesthesia machine if needed. Why: Avoids patient risk during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Power Supply
steps.instructions: Verify machine is plugged into a verified, live hospital outlet. Confirm battery (if present) is charged and installed correctly. Expected: Power-on sequence initiates without errors.

steps.title: Verify Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Check ambient temperature, humidity, and airflow around the machine. Ensure no obstructions around vents or oxygen supply lines. Why: Extreme conditions can cause self-test failures.

steps.title: Inspect Gas and Electrical Connections
steps.instructions: Confirm oxygen, air, and vacuum lines are connected and pressurized. Verify electrical connections and grounding. Expected: All lines and indicators show normal pressure and connectivity.

steps.title: Check Accessories and Sensors
steps.instructions: Ensure vaporizers are seated and recognized. Check breathing circuit, CO₂, O₂, and pressure sensors for correct installation. Expected: Machine recognizes all modules without error.

steps.title: Run Self-Test Again
steps.instructions: Power cycle the machine and initiate the system self-test or pre-use check. Observe the display for error codes or warnings. Expected: Self-test completes successfully.

steps.title: Document Error Codes
steps.instructions: Record any error codes, beeps, or indicator lights for further reference.

CCR.Complaint: Primus IE failed pre-use check with system error displayed.
CCR.Cause: Self-test failure due to unrecognized vaporizer module during setup.
CCR.Resolution: Verified connections, reseated vaporizer, and reran self-test successfully. Device returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Any error codes or abnormal sounds recorded

title: Drager Primus IE Anesthesia Machine - Agent Delivery or Vaporizer Recognition Errors
description: Identifies causes for vaporizer not recognized, agent delivery failure, or inaccurate readings before patient exposure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is connected to the device. Move the patient to an alternate anesthesia machine if needed.

steps.title: Verify Machine Power
steps.instructions: Ensure the anesthesia machine is powered on and running its initial self-test without error. A stable power source prevents false vaporizer errors.

steps.title: Check Vaporizer Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure the vaporizer is correctly seated in its mounting slot. Confirm the locking mechanism engages fully. Expected Outcome: Machine should detect the vaporizer. Misalignment often prevents recognition.

steps.title: Inspect Vaporizer Type and Compatibility
steps.instructions: Verify the vaporizer matches the Primus IE’s approved agent list. Cross-check model and fill capacity markings. Expected Outcome: Only compatible vaporizers are recognized; incompatible types can trigger errors.

steps.title: Check Vaporizer Filling and Level Sensor
steps.instructions: Ensure the agent level is sufficient. Look for leaks or spilled agent around the connection port. Expected Outcome: Low or empty vaporizer can cause the machine to fail recognition.

steps.title: Examine Sensor Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect electrical or mechanical connectors on the vaporizer and machine for dirt, debris, or corrosion. Clean connectors carefully if needed. Expected Outcome: Clean, secure connectors restore proper communication.

steps.title: Reset the Machine
steps.instructions: Power down, wait 30 seconds, then power up. Re-run the pre-use check or self-test. Expected Outcome: Temporary communication faults may clear.

steps.title: Swap with a Known Good Vaporizer
steps.instructions: Replace the suspected vaporizer with a verified working one. Expected Outcome: If recognition succeeds, the original vaporizer may be defective.

CCR.Complaint: Primus IE fails to recognize sevoflurane vaporizer during pre-use check.
CCR.Cause: Vaporizer misaligned and connector pins partially obstructed.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected seating, cleaned connectors, pre-use check completed successfully.

helpfulDetails: Machine self-test status and error codes
Any unusual clicks, resistance, or odors during installation

title: Drager Primus IE Anesthesia Machine - Fresh Gas Flow Control Malfunction
description: Identifies causes for inaccurate, erratic, or unresponsive fresh gas flow due to power, connection, or control system issues before patient use.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is connected to the machine. Move the patient to another anesthesia workstation if needed.

steps.title: Verify Power Supply
steps.instructions: Ensure the machine is plugged into a verified, live outlet. Check the main power switch and battery status. A stable power source is required for accurate flow control.

steps.title: Check Gas Supply Lines
steps.instructions: Inspect oxygen, air, and nitrous oxide lines for kinks, leaks, or disconnections. Verify cylinder pressure or central supply pressure is within normal range. Incorrect supply pressure can trigger flow errors.

steps.title: Inspect Flowmeter and Vaporizer Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure all flowmeter knobs move smoothly and correspond to changes on the display. Check that vaporizers are properly seated and recognized by the machine. Misalignment or poor contact can prevent correct flow measurement.

steps.title: Review Fresh Gas Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm that flow rates on the display match the settings on the control knobs. Reset settings to default if discrepancies are observed. Software or calibration mismatch may cause flow control malfunction.

steps.title: Check for Error Messages
steps.instructions: Observe the display for specific alarms or messages related to FGF (fresh gas flow). Document any error codes for escalation.

steps.title: Restart the Machine
steps.instructions: Power cycle the anesthesia machine. Confirm whether flow control responds correctly after reboot. Temporary software glitches can often be cleared this way.

CCR.Complaint: Fresh gas flow display erratic and unresponsive during pre-use check.
CCR.Cause: No external leaks, vaporizers seated correctly, but flowmeter readings remain inconsistent.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to Biomed bench for evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Final device status marked Out of Service

title: Drager Primus IE Anesthesia Machine - Ventilator Bellows Not Cycling
description: Identifies causes for bellows failing to rise/fall, including power, circuit, or valve issues, before patient exposure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is connected. Move the patient to an alternate anesthesia machine if ventilation is required.

steps.title: Verify Power Supply
steps.instructions: Check that the machine is plugged into a functional outlet and turned on. Confirm main power indicator is illuminated. Reason: Bellows movement requires electrical and pneumatic power; without it, cycling cannot occur.

steps.title: Check Oxygen/Compressed Gas Supply
steps.instructions: Confirm oxygen and air cylinders or pipelines are on and delivering adequate pressure. Look for low-pressure alarms. Reason: Bellows rely on gas flow to drive inspiration/expiration.

steps.title: Inspect Breathing Circuit Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure the inspiratory and expiratory limbs are securely connected. Verify that the bellows chamber is properly seated in its housing. Reason: Leaks or misalignment prevent proper bellows cycling.

steps.title: Examine Ventilator Settings
steps.instructions: Check mode, tidal volume, respiratory rate, and pressure limits. Ensure “Manual” or “Standby” modes are not engaged. Reason: Incorrect settings can inhibit automatic bellows movement.

steps.title: Check for Active Alarms or Obstructions
steps.instructions: Inspect for occluded filters, patient valves, or airway obstructions. Reset any active alarms and attempt to initiate a test breath. Reason: The ventilator will inhibit bellows movement if obstruction or fault is detected.

steps.title: Perform a System Reset
steps.instructions: Power cycle the machine following manufacturer guidelines. Re-run the pre-use check if applicable. Reason: Transient errors may prevent bellows cycling.

CCR.Complaint: Bellows not cycling during pre-use check; patient unable to ventilate.
CCR.Cause: Breathing circuit properly connected; gas supply and power verified; issue persisted. Likely internal ventilator fault.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Out of Service

title: Drager Primus IE Anesthesia Machine - Display or Drager SmartPilot Errors
description: Identifies causes for display malfunctions, touchscreen errors, or SmartPilot integration issues before patient exposure or internal troubleshooting.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is connected to the machine. Move the patient to another anesthesia workstation if ventilation is required.

steps.title: Check Power and Connections
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is connected to a stable power source. Inspect power cords for damage or loose connections. Confirm battery (if present) is charged. Expected outcome: Device powers on with normal startup sequence.

steps.title: Inspect Display and Interface
steps.instructions: Check for visible cracks, unresponsive touchscreen areas, or flickering. Clean screen gently with manufacturer-approved wipes. Expected outcome: Display responds to touch or button input.

steps.title: Verify SmartPilot Integration
steps.instructions: Confirm SmartPilot module is correctly seated in the connector. Check communication cables between the anesthesia machine and SmartPilot. Expected outcome: Device recognizes SmartPilot module; no communication errors.

steps.title: Restart and Soft Reset
steps.instructions: Power down the machine completely, wait 30 seconds, and restart. Expected outcome: Display initializes normally; SmartPilot errors clear if caused by software glitch.

steps.title: Check for Software Alerts or Updates
steps.instructions: Review system messages or error codes on startup. If recent updates were applied, confirm installation completed successfully. Expected outcome: No persistent error messages; system operates normally.

CCR.Complaint: Display intermittently unresponsive; SmartPilot module not recognized.
CCR.Cause: Module connector seating checked; touchscreen and cables visually normal; errors persist after power cycle.
CCR.Resolution: Machine removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for repair/bench evaluation.

description: AIRWAY PRESSURE HIGH alarm indicating elevated airway pressure due to obstruction, kinked circuit, or incorrect alarm limits affecting safe ventilation.

steps.instructions: Evaluate tidal volume, flow, and PEEP; adjust if causing excessive pressure.

steps.title: Inspect Valves and Scavenging
steps.instructions: Check expiratory valve and scavenging system for blockage or malfunction.

steps.title: Run System Test
steps.instructions: Perform system self-test or leak test to confirm proper function.

CCR.Complaint: Device alarming 'AIRWAY PRESSURE HIGH !!!' with elevated airway pressure readings during ventilation.

steps.instructions: Immediately remove the machine from active patient use if ventilation is compromised. Transition to a backup anesthesia machine or manual ventilation.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the VENTILATOR FAIL !!! alarm is present and observe when it occurs, ventilator performance, and any related alarms.

steps.title: Check Ventilator Mounting and Seating
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator module is fully seated, properly mounted, and the cover is closed and latched.

steps.title: Inspect Ventilator Membrane and Seal
steps.instructions: Inspect for misalignment, debris, or damage and reseat the membrane before securing the cover.

steps.title: Check PEEP/PMAX Line Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the hose is securely connected, free of kinks, and without leaks.

steps.title: Inspect Breathing System for Leaks or Disconnections
steps.instructions: Check all hoses and fittings for proper connection and absence of leaks.

steps.title: Reset Ventilation Mode
steps.instructions: Place the machine in Standby mode and return to the previous ventilation mode.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears, return the device to service and verify stable operation.

CCR.Complaint: VENTILATOR FAIL !!! alarm present. Ventilator not initiating or maintaining mechanical ventilation.
CCR.Cause: Loose ventilator mounting / disconnected PEEP line or internal ventilator module failure indicated after external checks.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated ventilator module, secured membrane and cover, reconnected PEEP/PMAX line. If unresolved, removed from service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: When alarm occurs (startup vs during ventilation)

title: Drager Fabius GS Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: VOLUME ALARMS OFF !
description: Volume alarms are disabled, removing alerts for abnormal ventilation volumes and increasing patient safety risk.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Verify patient ventilation and monitoring. Enable alarms or move to backup monitoring if needed.

steps.title: Verify Alarm
steps.instructions: Confirm VOLUME ALARMS OFF ! is displayed and alarms are disabled in settings.

steps.title: Check Alarm Settings
steps.instructions: Access alarm settings and confirm volume alarms are turned off.

steps.title: Reactivate Alarms
steps.instructions: Turn on volume alarms and set appropriate limits.

steps.title: Verify Functionality
steps.instructions: Confirm alarms trigger when limits are exceeded.

steps.title: Check User Behavior
steps.instructions: Determine if alarms were intentionally disabled and address workflow issues.

CCR.Complaint: VOLUME ALARMS OFF ! displayed; no volume alarms active during ventilation.
CCR.Cause: Volume alarms disabled in system settings due to user configuration.
CCR.Resolution: Re-enabled volume alarms, adjusted limits, and verified proper alarm functionality.

title: Drager Oxylog 3000 Plus Ventilator - Pre-Use Check or Leak Test Failure
description: Troubleshooting pre-use check or leak test failures caused by circuit leaks, loose connections, or setup issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from patient use before troubleshooting. Use a backup ventilator if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Run the pre-use check again and observe the exact failure message. Check for leak test failure, volume loss warnings, or specific component prompts.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Circuit Assembly
steps.instructions: Ensure all tubing connections are fully seated. Look for cracks, tears, or loose fittings. Confirm proper assembly per configuration.

steps.title: Check the Test Lung and Accessories
steps.instructions: Confirm test lung is properly attached. Ensure no open ports or missing caps. Replace test lung if suspected faulty.

steps.title: Inspect Filters, Water Traps, and Valves
steps.instructions: Ensure filters are seated correctly. Check water traps for proper sealing. Verify valves are installed and not loose.

steps.title: Check Inspiratory and Expiratory Ports
steps.instructions: Ensure circuit is firmly connected to both ports. Check for damaged seals or O-rings. Remove and reseat connections.

steps.title: Replace the Circuit with a Known Good Setup
steps.instructions: Use a new or verified working circuit. Re-run the pre-use check.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Setup Errors
steps.instructions: Ensure all ports are capped if not in use. Verify correct mode/setup for pre-use check. Confirm no additional devices are connected improperly.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator failed pre-use check with leak test error.
CCR.Cause: Leak identified in patient circuit due to loose expiratory limb connection.
CCR.Resolution: Secured connections and replaced circuit; pre-use check passed successfully.

helpfulDetails: Whether issue resolved with circuit replacement

title: Drager Oxylog 3000 Plus Ventilator - Gas Supply Pressure Alarms
description: Troubleshooting gas supply pressure alarms caused by gas source, hose, or connection issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately confirm the patient is being ventilated safely. If needed, switch to another ventilator or use a manual resuscitation bag.

steps.title: Verify Gas Source Availability
steps.instructions: Confirm connection to wall oxygen or cylinder. Ensure cylinder is not empty and verify wall outlet is active.

steps.title: Check Supply Pressure at Source
steps.instructions: Check wall outlet pressure using a known working device or gauge. Inspect cylinder pressure gauge.

steps.title: Inspect Gas Hose and Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure hose is securely connected, check for kinks or leaks, and swap with a known good hose if available.

steps.title: Check for Incorrect Gas Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm oxygen hose is connected to the correct outlet and verify color coding and labeling.

steps.title: Inspect Internal Filter/Water Trap (External Access Only)
steps.instructions: Inspect external filters or inlet ports for debris or moisture and ensure connectors are clean and dry.

steps.title: Restart the Ventilator
steps.instructions: Power off the ventilator, disconnect and reconnect gas supply, and restart to observe for alarms.

steps.title: Test with Alternate Gas Source
steps.instructions: Move to a known good outlet or use a different cylinder or regulator to verify source integrity.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator alarming for low gas supply pressure during use.
CCR.Cause: Cracked oxygen supply hose causing pressure drop.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced hose and verified normal operation with no further alarms.

title: Drager Oxylog 3000 Plus Ventilator - Flow sensor errors or calibration failure
description: Flow sensor errors, failed calibration, or inaccurate ventilation readings caused by contamination, improper assembly, or sensor-related external issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
Remove the ventilator from patient use and transition the patient to a functioning ventilator before troubleshooting.

Expected: Patient ventilation continues safely on an alternate device.
Why it matters: Flow measurement errors can result in incorrect tidal volumes and unsafe ventilation.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions:
Power on the ventilator and observe the exact error message or calibration failure.

Check:
- Specific flow sensor or calibration error message
- Whether error occurs during startup or operation

Expected: Clear identification of the error condition.
Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is repeatable and sensor-related.

steps.title: Inspect Flow Sensor Assembly
steps.instructions:
Locate and inspect the flow sensor and associated components.

Actions:
- Ensure the flow sensor is properly seated and connected
- Check for visible contamination (moisture, secretions, debris)
- Confirm correct orientation per manufacturer design

Expected: Clean, dry, and properly installed sensor.
Why it matters: Misalignment or contamination is a common cause of sensor errors.

steps.title: Check Patient Circuit and Accessories
steps.instructions:
Inspect the breathing circuit connected to the ventilator.

Actions:
- Verify correct circuit setup (no missing or incorrect components)
- Check for condensation or water buildup in tubing
- Replace HME/filter if saturated
- Ensure no kinks or obstructions

Expected: Dry, unobstructed circuit with proper components.
Why it matters: Excess moisture or restriction affects flow readings and calibration.

steps.title: Perform Flow Sensor Cleaning (If Applicable)
steps.instructions:
If contamination is present, clean the flow sensor per hospital protocol.

Actions:
- Remove the sensor
- Clean and dry thoroughly (no residual moisture)
- Reinstall securely

Expected: Sensor is clean and completely dry before reuse.
Why it matters: Even small amounts of moisture can disrupt flow measurement accuracy.

steps.title: Repeat Calibration Procedure
steps.instructions:
Initiate the ventilator’s calibration or self-test process.

Actions:
- Ensure no patient is connected
- Follow on-screen calibration prompts
- Allow full completion without interruption

Expected: Calibration completes successfully with no errors.
Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue was due to external or setup-related causes.

steps.title: Swap with Known Good Flow Sensor (If Available)
steps.instructions:
If the issue persists, replace the flow sensor with a known good unit.

Expected: Device calibrates successfully with replacement sensor.
Why it matters: Helps isolate whether the original sensor is faulty.

steps.title: Check Connector and Port Condition
steps.instructions:
Inspect the connection point where the flow sensor interfaces with the ventilator.

Actions:
- Look for bent pins, corrosion, or debris
- Ensure secure and proper connection

Expected: Clean, intact connection interface.
Why it matters: Poor electrical or mechanical connection can trigger sensor errors.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator displaying flow sensor error and failing calibration during startup.
CCR.Cause: Contaminated flow sensor with moisture present affecting calibration.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned and dried flow sensor, reinstalled, and successfully completed calibration test.

helpfulDetails: Presence of moisture or debris in sensor
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Drager Oxylog 3000 Plus Ventilator - Battery Not Charging or Shortened Runtime
description: Troubleshooting battery charging issues and reduced runtime caused by power source, battery condition, or connection problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the ventilator is in use, immediately transition the patient to another ventilator before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify External Power Source
steps.instructions: Plug in a known working device to confirm outlet power, try a different outlet, and verify vehicle power if using DC source.

steps.title: Inspect AC/DC Power Adapter and Cable
steps.instructions: Ensure connections are fully seated, inspect for damage, and swap with a known good adapter if available.

steps.title: Check Charging Indicators on the Ventilator
steps.instructions: Confirm charging icon or LED is present and note any error messages or absence of indication.

steps.title: Verify Battery Installation and Seating
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the battery, ensure clean contacts, and inspect for corrosion or debris.

steps.title: Evaluate Battery Condition and Age
steps.instructions: Review battery age, note shortened runtime, and test with a known good battery if available.

steps.title: Perform Functional Runtime Test
steps.instructions: Fully charge if possible, disconnect AC power, and monitor runtime under normal operation.

steps.title: Check for Overheating or Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure vents are clear and check for excessive heat during operation or charging.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator not charging and losing power quickly during transport use.
CCR.Cause: Degraded battery with significantly reduced runtime confirmed during functional testing.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced battery and verified normal charging and runtime operation.

title: Drager Oxylog 3000 Plus Ventilator - Alarm System or Audio Failure
description: Troubleshooting no audible alarms, low volume, or missing alert tones due to settings, obstruction, or configuration issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not rely on a ventilator with non-functional alarms. Immediately move the patient to a fully functional ventilator if alarms are not audible.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Trigger a test alarm (disconnect circuit briefly or use alarm test if available) and check for no sound, low volume, or distorted sound.

steps.title: Check Alarm Volume Settings
steps.instructions: Navigate to alarm settings, ensure volume is not muted or low, and increase to maximum to retest.

steps.title: Inspect for Speaker Obstruction
steps.instructions: Check for tape, dust, or debris blocking the speaker and remove or clean as needed.

steps.title: Check for External Audio Routing or Accessories
steps.instructions: Disconnect external alarm cables, verify no silent mode is active, and confirm alarms are not routed externally.

steps.title: Perform a Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Power off the unit, disconnect briefly, restart, and retest alarm functionality.

steps.title: Check for Alarm Indicators (Visual vs Audible)
steps.instructions: Trigger an alarm and confirm whether visual alarms are present even if audio is missing.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator alarms not audible during use.
CCR.Cause: Internal speaker failure after volume and configuration verified.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent to Biomed shop for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Any distortion or intermittent audio noted
Final device status (removed from service or resolved)

steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the Vamos 2 on an active patient if CO₂ monitoring is required for anesthesia care. Confirm the patient is being monitored safely. Verify ventilation using the anesthesia machine, reservoir bag movement, chest rise, SpO₂, airway pressure, and any alternate EtCO₂ source if available. If EtCO₂ is clinically required and the Vamos 2 is not displaying CO₂, move the patient to another verified monitor or module before continuing. Expected outcome: The patient remains safely monitored while the CO₂ issue is investigated. Why it matters: Loss of CO₂ monitoring during anesthesia can delay recognition of apnea, disconnection, airway obstruction, or ventilation problems. If patient safety is restored by using another monitor, continue troubleshooting the affected Vamos 2 off the patient.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what is missing. Check whether the monitor shows: No CO₂ waveform, No EtCO₂ numeric value, A flat CO₂ waveform, A dashed or blank CO₂ value, A sampling-line or gas measurement alarm, A startup or warm-up message. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as a CO₂ measurement problem rather than a display, alarm limit, or patient setup issue. If CO₂ appears after normal startup or warm-up, verify operation and stop.

steps.title: Check That CO₂ Monitoring Is Enabled and Displayed
steps.instructions: Confirm the Vamos 2 is configured to show the CO₂ parameter. Check the screen layout and parameter display. Verify CO₂ has not been hidden, disabled, or removed from the active view. Confirm the unit is in the correct monitoring mode for the clinical area. Expected outcome: CO₂ waveform and numeric fields are visible on the display. If CO₂ was hidden and restoring the display resolves the issue, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Sampling Line Connection
steps.instructions: A missing CO₂ reading is commonly caused by an open, disconnected, kinked, or blocked sample line. Check that the sampling line is firmly connected to the Vamos 2 gas sample inlet. Check the patient end of the sampling line or airway adapter connection. Verify the line is not loose, cracked, stretched, kinked, pinched, or disconnected. Expected outcome: The sampling path is fully connected from the airway circuit to the monitor. If reconnecting or replacing the line restores CO₂, verify stable waveform and stop.

steps.title: Replace the Sampling Line
steps.instructions: Even if the sampling line looks normal, it may be blocked internally by moisture, secretions, or debris. Remove the current sampling line. Install a known-good compatible sampling line. Ensure the replacement line seats correctly at the monitor and airway connection. Allow the monitor time to draw a sample and update the CO₂ reading. Expected outcome: CO₂ waveform and EtCO₂ numeric value return. If a new line resolves the issue, the original sampling line was likely blocked, leaking, or damaged. Stop and document the accessory replacement.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Contamination
steps.instructions: Moisture in the sample path can prevent proper gas measurement. Inspect the sampling line, water trap if present, and sample inlet area for visible fluid, condensation, or contamination. Replace wet or contaminated disposable accessories. Do not force liquid into the monitor. Do not blow compressed air into the gas inlet unless approved by facility procedure and manufacturer guidance. Expected outcome: The gas sample path is dry, clean, and unobstructed. If removing wet accessories restores CO₂, verify stable operation and stop.

steps.title: Verify the Airway Adapter or Sample Port
steps.instructions: A proper airway sample point is required for sidestream CO₂ measurement. Check that the sampling line is connected to the correct airway adapter or sample port. Confirm the adapter is not installed backward, blocked, capped, cracked, or disconnected from the breathing circuit. Verify the sample port is exposed to exhaled gas and not connected to the wrong circuit location. Expected outcome: The Vamos 2 receives a valid airway gas sample. If correcting the adapter or sample location restores CO₂, document the setup issue and stop.

steps.title: Check for Circuit Leak or No Exhaled Sample
steps.instructions: The monitor cannot display CO₂ if it is not receiving exhaled gas. Confirm ventilation is occurring. Check for a disconnected breathing circuit, loose airway connection, open sampling port, leaking adapter, or excessive circuit leak. Confirm the patient is not disconnected from the anesthesia circuit. Expected outcome: A valid exhaled gas sample reaches the sampling line. If the CO₂ reading returns after correcting a circuit or adapter issue, stop and document the external cause.

steps.title: Check the Sample Inlet and Filter Area
steps.instructions: The monitor-side gas inlet or filter area may be blocked or improperly seated. Inspect the sample inlet connection for damage, debris, or loose fit. Confirm any required filter, water trap, or connector is installed correctly. Remove and reseat the accessory if appropriate. Replace questionable disposable components with known-good compatible parts. Expected outcome: The monitor can pull a gas sample without obstruction or leakage. If reseating or replacing the inlet accessory restores CO₂, verify the reading and stop.

steps.title: Listen for Sampling Pump Activity
steps.instructions: A sidestream gas monitor generally requires sample flow through the unit. With the monitor powered on and gas monitoring active, listen for normal sampling pump activity if it is normally audible on this device. Check for alarms related to sample flow, blocked line, or gas measurement failure. Compare behavior to a known-good unit if available. Expected outcome: The monitor appears to be drawing sample normally. If there is no apparent sample flow and external accessories have already been ruled out, remove the device from service.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor When Safe
steps.instructions: A temporary software or initialization issue may prevent the CO₂ parameter from updating. Only perform a restart when the device is not being used for active patient monitoring. Power the Vamos 2 off. Wait briefly. Power it back on and allow the gas measurement system to initialize. Reconnect a known-good sampling line and verify CO₂ function. Expected outcome: CO₂ measurement initializes normally. If CO₂ returns after restart, perform a functional check before returning the monitor to service.

steps.title: Compare With a Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions: Use comparison testing to separate monitor failure from accessory or setup failure. Use a known-good sampling line, airway adapter, and test setup. If available, compare with another working gas monitor using the same external accessories. Verify whether the issue follows the accessory setup or stays with the Vamos 2. Expected outcome: The fault is isolated to either the external setup or the monitor itself. If the issue follows the accessories, replace the faulty accessory. If the issue stays with the Vamos 2, remove the monitor from service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Drager Vamos 2 anesthesia monitor displayed no CO₂ reading during setup.
CCR.Cause: Found the CO₂ sampling line connected but blocked with moisture, preventing the monitor from drawing a valid gas sample.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the sampling line, verified EtCO₂ waveform and numeric reading returned, and confirmed the monitor operated normally before returning it to service.

helpfulDetails: Water trap or filter condition checked, if applicable
Whether waveform, numeric value, or both were missing

title: Drager Vamos 2 Anesthesia Monitor - Agent Identification Failure
description: Troubleshooting agent identification failure caused by sampling, gas flow, setup, contamination, or monitor faults.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting the Vamos 2, confirm the anesthesia provider has reliable gas and agent monitoring available. Action: Verify the patient is being monitored safely. Confirm whether another anesthesia gas monitor, anesthesia machine gas module, or backup monitoring method is available. Do not interrupt active anesthetic monitoring unless the anesthesia provider confirms it is safe. If agent identification is unreliable during a case, notify anesthesia immediately. Expected outcome: The patient remains safely monitored while the agent identification issue is evaluated. Why it matters: Agent identification failure can affect anesthetic agent awareness, MAC display, inspired/expired agent values, and clinical decision-making. If this resolves the concern by moving monitoring to a reliable device, stop and document the action.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact symptom before replacing parts or removing the device. Action: Check whether the Vamos 2 displays: Agent identification failure. Agent not identified. Incorrect agent. No agent value. Intermittent agent reading. Observe whether CO2, N2O, O2, or other gas values are still displaying normally. Expected outcome: The failure is confirmed as an agent identification issue, not a full gas sampling failure. Why it matters: If all gas values are missing, the problem may be with sample flow, the sample line, the water trap, or the pump rather than only agent identification.

steps.title: 3. Check the Sample Line Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the sample line from the breathing circuit to the Vamos 2. Action: Confirm the sample line is connected securely at both ends. Check for loose fittings, cracks, kinks, crushed tubing, or disconnection. Replace the sample line with a known-good line if damage or restriction is suspected. Expected outcome: Sample gas reaches the monitor without leaks or restrictions. If agent identification returns after correcting the sample line, stop and document the issue.

steps.title: 4. Check the Water Trap
steps.instructions: Inspect the water trap or moisture collection point. Action: Verify the water trap is properly seated. Check for moisture, secretions, contamination, or blockage. Replace the water trap if it appears wet, dirty, cracked, loose, or overdue for replacement. Expected outcome: The sample path is dry, sealed, and unobstructed. Why it matters: Moisture or contamination in the sample path can interfere with accurate gas and agent measurement. If replacing or reseating the water trap restores agent identification, stop and document the correction.

steps.title: 5. Verify Sample Flow
steps.instructions: Check whether the Vamos 2 appears to be pulling sample gas correctly. Action: Look for sample flow alarms, occlusion messages, or weak/no sampling behavior. Listen for abnormal pump noise if normally audible. Confirm the sample line is not connected to a blocked filter, capped port, or incorrect connection point. Expected outcome: The monitor has stable sample flow and no obvious occlusion. If sample flow is not present or cannot be restored with external checks, remove the device from service.

steps.title: 6. Confirm the Anesthetic Agent Is Actually Present
steps.instructions: Make sure the issue is not caused by the clinical setup. Action: Ask anesthesia which agent is in use. Confirm the vaporizer is turned on only if clinically appropriate and verified by anesthesia. Confirm the monitor has had enough time to detect the agent after fresh gas flow begins. Do not adjust anesthesia machine settings yourself unless you are qualified and directed by anesthesia. Expected outcome: The agent identification failure is not caused by absence of agent or a timing/setup issue. Why it matters: Some monitors may need a stable sample before identifying the agent correctly.

steps.title: 7. Check for Mixed or Changing Agents
steps.instructions: Agent identification may fail or become unreliable if multiple agents are present or the system is transitioning between agents. Action: Ask whether the case recently switched agents. Ask whether the breathing circuit or anesthesia machine may contain residual agent from a previous case. Allow the monitor to observe a stable gas sample if clinically appropriate. Expected outcome: The monitor identifies the agent once the sampled gas stabilizes. If the issue clears after the agent concentration stabilizes, stop and document the finding.

steps.title: 8. Inspect for Contamination or Unusual Odor
steps.instructions: Check for evidence of contamination around the sample system. Action: Inspect the sample line, trap, fittings, and monitor inlet. Look for liquid, residue, discoloration, chemical odor, or visible contamination. Do not continue using a contaminated monitor on a patient. Expected outcome: The external sampling path is clean and safe to use. If contamination is found, remove the device from service for cleaning, evaluation, and possible repair.

steps.title: 9. Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the device is not being used for active patient monitoring, perform a controlled restart. Action: Confirm with anesthesia that the device can be safely restarted. Power the Vamos 2 off. Wait briefly, then power it back on. Allow the device to complete startup and self-check. Reconnect the sample line and observe whether agent identification returns. Expected outcome: A temporary software or startup recognition issue clears. If the issue returns after restart, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: 10. Compare Against a Known-Good Monitor
steps.instructions: If available, compare the Vamos 2 to another anesthesia gas monitor or gas module. Action: Use the same clinical setup only if safe and approved by anesthesia. Confirm whether the other monitor identifies the agent correctly. If the known-good monitor works with the same sample setup, suspect the Vamos 2. Expected outcome: The issue is isolated to either the external sampling setup or the Vamos 2 itself. Why it matters: A known-good comparison helps avoid unnecessary repair when the problem is actually the sample line, water trap, or anesthesia setup.

steps.title: 11. Check Date, Service Status, and Calibration/PM History
steps.instructions: Review the device’s maintenance status. Action: Check whether the Vamos 2 is overdue for PM, gas calibration, or manufacturer-recommended service. Review recent work orders for repeat agent identification complaints. Confirm whether the failure is recurring across multiple rooms or cases. Expected outcome: Repeat or overdue-service issues are identified before returning the monitor to use. If the monitor is overdue or has repeated failures, remove it from service for bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Anesthesia reported that the Drager Vamos 2 failed to identify the anesthetic agent during use.
CCR.Cause: Found the water trap not fully seated, causing unreliable sample flow and intermittent agent identification.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the water trap, replaced the sample line, verified stable sample flow, and confirmed agent identification returned during functional check.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested if power concerns were involved
Water trap condition and seating checked
Any sample flow or occlusion messages observed
Whether CO2 and other gas values were present
Agent being used, if provided by anesthesia
Whether the issue was intermittent or constant
Whether another monitor identified the agent correctly
Any unusual odor, moisture, residue, or contamination
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Drager Vamos 2 Anesthesia Monitor - Sampling System Occlusion
description: Troubleshooting a Vamos 2 sampling system occlusion caused by blocked sample lines, water traps, filters, or exhaust restrictions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the sampling system on an active patient unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. Confirm anesthesia gas, oxygenation, ventilation, and patient monitoring are being maintained. If gas monitoring is required and the Vamos 2 is not providing reliable readings, have clinical staff use another verified gas monitor or anesthesia system module. Do not delay patient care while investigating the sampling occlusion. Expected outcome: The patient remains safely monitored while the sampling issue is evaluated. Why it matters: A sampling occlusion may prevent accurate CO₂, O₂, N₂O, or anesthetic agent readings. If this resolves the safety concern, continue troubleshooting only when safe.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Occlusion
steps.instructions: Confirm the actual message, alarm, or behavior reported. Check whether the Vamos 2 displays a sampling system occlusion, sample line blocked condition, low/no sample flow, or missing gas readings. Observe whether CO₂ and agent values are absent, delayed, unstable, or not updating. Listen for abnormal pump sounds, repeated cycling, or unusual restriction noises. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as a sampling flow problem rather than a display, communication, or sensor interpretation issue.

steps.title: Check the Sample Line for Kinks or Blockage
steps.instructions: Inspect the full length of the gas sample line. Look for kinks, pinched tubing, crushed sections, liquid in the line, secretions or contamination, and a blocked Luer connection. Replace the sample line with a known-good compatible line if any restriction is found. Expected outcome: Sample flow returns and gas readings stabilize. If the occlusion clears, document the blocked or damaged sample line and stop.

steps.title: Verify the Sample Line Is Connected Correctly
steps.instructions: Check the connection between the patient circuit, sample line, water trap, and Vamos 2 inlet. Confirm the sample line is connected to the correct sampling port. Confirm the Luer fitting is tight but not cross-threaded. Check that the sample line is not connected to a capped or blocked adapter. Verify the sampling point on the airway adapter is open and positioned correctly. Expected outcome: The monitor can draw gas from the breathing circuit without restriction. If reconnection restores readings, document the connection issue and stop.

steps.title: Check the Airway Adapter or Sampling Port
steps.instructions: Inspect the airway adapter or gas sampling connection at the breathing circuit. Look for moisture, secretions, condensation, or debris blocking the sampling port. Replace the airway adapter or sampling connector if contamination is present. Confirm the replacement part is compatible and properly installed. Expected outcome: Gas sample flow is restored through the patient circuit sampling point. If the issue clears after replacing the airway adapter, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect the Water Trap
steps.instructions: Check the water trap for fullness, blockage, improper seating, or contamination. Remove the water trap only when safe and appropriate. Check whether it is full, wet, clogged, cracked, or contaminated. Empty or replace it according to facility procedure. Reseat the water trap firmly and confirm it locks into place. Expected outcome: The sampling system recognizes the water trap and draws normally. Why it matters: A full or blocked water trap is one of the most common external causes of sampling occlusion. If the occlusion clears after water trap service, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Check for Incorrect or Non-Compatible Consumables
steps.instructions: Verify the sample line, water trap, filter, and airway adapter are compatible with the Vamos 2 sampling system. Look for recently changed accessories. Compare the installed consumables with approved hospital stock. Replace questionable accessories with known-good compatible parts. Expected outcome: The sampling path is restored using parts that allow proper flow and moisture management. If replacing consumables resolves the issue, document the incompatible or defective accessory and stop.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Liquid Contamination
steps.instructions: Look for signs that fluid has entered the sample path. Inspect the sample line, water trap, and inlet area for visible liquid. Replace wet sample lines and contaminated disposable components. Allow the external inlet area to dry if only minor external moisture is present. Do not blow compressed air into the device unless directed by approved service procedure. Expected outcome: External fluid contamination is corrected without forcing liquid deeper into the monitor. If fluid appears to have entered the internal sampling system, remove the device from service.

steps.title: Check the Exhaust Path
