steps.instructions: Verify the gas exhaust path is not blocked. Inspect the exhaust tubing if connected. Check for kinked, capped, crushed, or liquid-filled exhaust tubing. Confirm scavenging or exhaust connections are not creating excessive back pressure. Disconnect or replace questionable exhaust tubing if safe and appropriate. Expected outcome: The Vamos 2 can draw and exhaust sample gas normally. If clearing the exhaust restriction resolves the occlusion, document the blocked exhaust path and stop.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Recheck
steps.instructions: After external restrictions are corrected, restart the monitor if the occlusion message remains. Remove the device from active patient use first. Power the Vamos 2 down. Wait briefly, then power it back on. Allow the monitor to complete startup and sampling checks. Reconnect a known-good sample line and water trap. Expected outcome: The occlusion alarm clears and gas readings return. If the alarm returns immediately with known-good external components, suspect an internal sampling system issue.

steps.title: Compare With Known-Good Accessories
steps.instructions: Use a known-good complete sampling setup before condemning the monitor. Install a known-good sample line. Install a known-good water trap. Use a known-good airway adapter or test sampling source if available. Confirm the exhaust path is open. Expected outcome: The Vamos 2 samples normally with verified accessories. If the occlusion remains with all external parts replaced, external causes have likely been ruled out.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Vamos 2 anesthesia monitor reported sampling system occlusion and was not displaying reliable gas readings.
CCR.Cause: Found the gas sample line kinked near the airway adapter, restricting sample flow to the monitor.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the sample line, verified the water trap was seated correctly, confirmed normal sample flow and gas readings, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested if power was questioned
CO₂ or agent readings present or absent
Any moisture, secretions, or contamination found
Environmental factors near the device or sampling setup
Indicator lights, startup messages, or sampling status behavior

title: Drager Vamos 2 Anesthesia Monitor - Measurement Drift or Calibration Issue
description: Troubleshooting drifting gas measurements, unstable readings, or calibration-related issues before sending the monitor for repair.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot a suspected measurement accuracy issue on an active patient unless safe alternate monitoring is already in place. Remove the Vamos 2 from active anesthesia use if readings are drifting, unstable, or not clinically believable. Confirm the patient is being monitored by another verified gas analyzer or anesthesia monitor if monitoring is still required. Notify anesthesia staff that the device should not be trusted until verified. Expected outcome: Patient safety is protected before troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: A drifting anesthesia gas or CO₂ measurement can affect ventilation assessment, agent delivery awareness, and clinical decision-making. If this resolves the immediate safety concern, continue troubleshooting off-patient.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Measurement Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what is drifting or failing calibration. Ask staff what parameter was affected, such as end-tidal CO₂, inspired CO₂, O₂, N₂O, anesthetic agent concentration, or agent identification. Observe whether the reading is slowly drifting upward or downward. Check whether the reading is intermittently unstable. Check whether the reading is flatlined. Compare whether the reading is different from another monitor. Confirm whether the device is failing a calibration or zeroing process. Expected outcome: The reported issue is clearly identified before parts or accessories are changed. Why it matters: “Calibration issue” can be caused by the monitor, sampling line, water trap, gas source, contamination, or incorrect setup.

steps.title: Check for Active Alarms or Status Messages
steps.instructions: Look for occlusion, sampling, calibration, zeroing, or gas measurement messages. Review the Vamos 2 display for any active messages related to sample line occlusion. Check for water trap issue messages. Check for calibration needed messages. Check for zeroing failure messages. Check for gas measurement unavailable messages. Check for agent identification failure messages. Check for pump or flow issue messages. Expected outcome: Any displayed alarm helps narrow the cause. If the monitor shows a sampling system or occlusion alarm, troubleshoot the sampling path before assuming calibration failure.

steps.title: Inspect the Sampling Line
steps.instructions: Check the sample line from the patient connection to the Vamos 2. Look for kinks. Look for moisture. Look for secretions. Look for crushed tubing. Look for loose luer connections. Check for incorrect sample line type. Check for a blocked filter or connector. Replace the sample line with a known-good compatible line. Expected outcome: Gas readings stabilize after replacing a restricted or contaminated sample line. Why it matters: Partial restriction or contamination can cause delayed response, incorrect values, or drifting measurements. If readings stabilize, document the faulty sample line and return the monitor to service after verification.

steps.title: Check the Water Trap
steps.instructions: Inspect the water trap for fluid, contamination, improper seating, or age-related issues. Remove and inspect the water trap. Empty or replace it according to facility procedure. Confirm it is fully seated and locked into place. Check for cracks, missing seals, or visible contamination. Expected outcome: The monitor samples normally and measurements become stable. Why it matters: A leaking or saturated water trap can affect sample flow and measurement accuracy. If the issue clears after replacing or reseating the water trap, stop and document the accessory-related cause.

steps.title: Allow Proper Warm-Up Time
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor has had enough time to stabilize after power-up. Power the monitor on. Allow it to complete startup and warm-up. Do not judge gas accuracy immediately after power-on unless the device indicates it is ready. Expected outcome: Readings become stable after normal warm-up. Why it matters: Gas analyzers may require stabilization time before accurate measurement is available. If the drift disappears after warm-up, document that no fault was duplicated after stabilization.

steps.title: Verify the Sample Gas Source
steps.instructions: If bench testing, use a known-good gas source or simulator appropriate for the parameter being checked. Use a verified test gas or approved analyzer test setup if available. Confirm the gas cylinder is not expired, empty, mislabeled, or connected incorrectly. Compare readings only against a known-good reference. Expected outcome: The Vamos 2 reading matches the expected gas value within acceptable facility or manufacturer tolerance. Why it matters: A bad test source can make a good monitor appear out of calibration.

steps.title: Compare Against Another Known-Good Monitor
steps.instructions: Use a second verified analyzer when possible. Connect both monitors to the same appropriate test source or controlled sampling setup. Allow both devices to stabilize. Compare the readings. Expected outcome: The Vamos 2 either agrees with the reference monitor or shows a repeatable offset/drift. Why it matters: Comparison testing helps separate a true device issue from a setup or gas source issue. If the Vamos 2 agrees with the reference monitor, document no fault found and include the test conditions.

steps.title: Check for Leaks in the Sampling Path
steps.instructions: A small leak can dilute the sampled gas and cause low, unstable, or drifting values. Inspect all external fittings and sample connections. Confirm the water trap is properly installed. Check for loose adapters or damaged sample line connectors. Replace questionable external accessories. Expected outcome: Readings stabilize when the sampling path is sealed. Why it matters: Leaks can mimic calibration drift by allowing room air into the gas sample.

steps.title: Check for Contamination or Residual Moisture
steps.instructions: Moisture, residue, or contaminated accessories can cause slow response and measurement instability. Replace the sample line and water trap. Allow the monitor to sample clean room air or approved test gas. Observe whether readings return to expected baseline behavior. Expected outcome: The analyzer clears and readings stabilize. Why it matters: Contamination in the sampling path can cause false drift even when the internal analyzer is functioning.

steps.title: Perform User-Level Zeroing or Calibration Check Only if Allowed
steps.instructions: Use only facility-approved and manufacturer-supported procedures available to Clinical Engineering. Follow the hospital-approved procedure for any external zeroing, calibration check, or verification. Do not perform unauthorized internal calibration adjustments. Record the starting value, expected value, final value, and result. Expected outcome: The device passes verification or clearly fails in a repeatable way. Why it matters: Clinical Engineering should verify performance without creating an undocumented or unauthorized calibration change.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Recheck
steps.instructions: If the issue is intermittent, power cycle the monitor and retest. Turn the monitor off. Disconnect from patient use and external accessories. Reconnect known-good sampling accessories. Power the unit back on. Allow warm-up and repeat the measurement check. Expected outcome: The issue either clears or returns consistently. Why it matters: A repeatable failure is stronger evidence of an internal measurement or calibration problem.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Device Can Be Returned to Service
steps.instructions: Only return the monitor to service if the readings are stable and verified. Confirm no active gas measurement alarms remain. Confirm the sampling system operates normally. Confirm readings are stable. Confirm external accessories have been replaced or verified. Confirm the device passes the facility’s performance check. Expected outcome: The monitor is safe to return to clinical use. If measurement drift remains, do not return the device to service.

CCR.Complaint: Anesthesia reported the Drager Vamos 2 gas readings were drifting and did not match expected CO₂ and agent values during use.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found a contaminated water trap and moisture in the sampling line causing unstable gas measurement readings.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the water trap and sample line, allowed the monitor to warm up, verified stable readings with a known-good test setup, and returned the device to service.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior, including occlusion, calibration, zeroing, or gas measurement messages noted
Accessories swapped, including sampling line, water trap, filter, or connectors
Power behavior, including outlet tested, power cord condition checked, startup behavior, and warm-up time allowed
Environmental factors, including moisture, contamination, visible residue, or unusual pump noise
Indicator lights or displayed status messages observed during testing
Final device status, including whether readings were verified, no fault was found, returned to service, or removed from service

title: Drager Vamos 2 Anesthesia Monitor - Display Or Power Startup Fault
description: Troubleshooting a Vamos 2 that will not power on, has no display, freezes, or fails during startup.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the monitor while it is needed for active anesthesia monitoring. Remove the Vamos 2 from patient use if it fails to power on, has a blank display, freezes during startup, or cannot display gas values reliably. Use another verified anesthesia gas monitor or anesthesia machine monitor before continuing. Expected outcome: The patient is monitored safely on known-good equipment. Why it matters: A startup or display fault can prevent oxygen, CO₂, nitrous oxide, or anesthetic agent monitoring from being available during a case.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what the user is reporting. Check whether the issue is no power at all. Check whether the issue is a blank display with power indicator present. Check whether the issue is display flicker or dim screen. Check whether the issue is startup freeze. Check whether the issue is repeated rebooting. Check whether the issue is error during startup. Check whether the unit powers on but does not complete warm-up. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before troubleshooting continues. Why it matters: A dead unit, a display fault, and a startup software fault may look similar at first but lead to different troubleshooting paths.

steps.title: Check the AC Outlet
steps.instructions: Verify that the wall outlet or power strip is supplying power. Plug a known-good device or outlet tester into the same receptacle. Confirm the outlet is not controlled by a switched circuit. Avoid using damaged power strips, loose receptacles, or questionable OR boom outlets. Expected outcome: The outlet provides proper AC power. If the outlet is dead or unreliable, move the monitor to a known-good outlet and retest. If the monitor starts normally, the issue is external to the Vamos 2. Stop troubleshooting and document the outlet issue.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord
steps.instructions: Check the external power cord before suspecting the monitor. Inspect the cord for cuts, crushed insulation, bent blades, loose connectors, or signs of heat damage. Confirm the cord is fully seated at the monitor and wall outlet. Swap with a known-good compatible hospital-grade power cord if available. Expected outcome: The monitor powers on normally with a verified cord. If the cord is damaged or the problem follows the cord, replace the cord, verify operation, and return the monitor to service if all checks pass.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the monitor for signs of drop damage or liquid exposure. Look for cracked housing, damaged display glass, broken knobs or buttons, loose connectors, fluid residue, unusual smell, or rattling sounds. Check around the power entry area and display bezel. Expected outcome: No visible damage is found. If there is evidence of fluid intrusion, burning smell, unusual heat, or impact damage, remove the device from service and send it for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Confirm the Power Button or Switch Operation
steps.instructions: Verify that the power control is physically working. Press the power button or operate the power switch as normally required. Observe whether any indicator lights, fan noise, relay clicks, display backlight, or startup sounds occur. Expected outcome: The monitor shows a normal startup response. If the switch feels stuck, loose, damaged, or does not produce any response, remove the monitor from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Observe Startup Indicators
steps.instructions: Look for signs that the unit is receiving power even if the display appears blank. Check for power LED activity. Check for backlight glow. Check for startup screen. Check for audible beeps. Check for internal fan or airflow sound. Check for display flicker. Check for repeated restart behavior. Expected outcome: The startup behavior helps separate a total power failure from a display or software startup issue. If the unit appears powered but the screen stays blank, suspect display, backlight, internal power, or startup electronics after external checks are complete.

steps.title: Disconnect External Accessories and Retest
steps.instructions: External connections can occasionally interfere with startup or make a fault appear worse. Power the unit off. Disconnect nonessential accessories, network connections, printer connections, data cables, sample line, scavenging/exhaust tubing if applicable, and external peripherals. Reconnect only AC power and attempt startup again. Expected outcome: The Vamos 2 starts normally without external accessories connected. If the monitor starts normally, reconnect accessories one at a time to identify the item causing the fault. Replace the faulty accessory or cable if confirmed.

steps.title: Check Sampling System Load Only After Power Is Stable
steps.instructions: A sampling system issue is not usually the first cause of a dead display, but a blocked or fluid-contaminated system can contribute to startup or readiness problems. Inspect the water trap, sample line, and gas inlet path. Remove obvious kinks, blockage, fluid, or contamination. Install a clean, correct water trap and sample line if needed. Expected outcome: The monitor completes startup and sampling readiness without obstruction-related warnings. If the display or power fault remains unchanged, continue troubleshooting as a power/display/startup issue.

steps.title: Try a Controlled Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Perform a simple reset only if the unit is not connected to a patient and there are no signs of electrical damage. Turn the monitor off. Disconnect AC power. Wait briefly to allow the unit to fully shut down. Reconnect AC power. Power the unit back on. Expected outcome: The monitor boots normally and the display becomes usable. If the problem clears, allow the unit to complete startup and verify normal display, gas measurement readiness, alarms, and controls before returning it to service.

steps.title: Check for Overheating or Environmental Issues
steps.instructions: Startup faults may occur if the monitor is overheated or ventilation is blocked. Verify the unit is not covered by drapes, supplies, or stacked equipment. Check that ventilation openings are not blocked. Feel for unusual heat at the housing or power entry area. Listen for abnormal fan noise if applicable. Expected outcome: The monitor operates in a normal environment without excessive heat. If the unit is unusually hot, smells burnt, or shuts down again after cooling, remove it from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Normal Operation Before Return to Service
steps.instructions: If the monitor starts normally after external troubleshooting, confirm it is safe to use. Verify display is bright and stable. Verify controls respond normally. Verify startup completes without freezing. Verify no repeated rebooting occurs. Verify no active power or system alarms remain. Verify sampling system is clear. Verify gas readings respond appropriately during functional check. Expected outcome: The monitor passes a basic operational check and is ready for clinical use. If any abnormal behavior remains, do not return the monitor to service.

CCR.Complaint: OR staff reported that the Drager Vamos 2 anesthesia monitor would not complete startup and the display stayed blank after power-on.
CCR.Cause: Verified wall outlet and power cord were functional, external accessories were disconnected, and the monitor still failed to display a normal startup screen.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the Vamos 2 from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for bench evaluation due to suspected internal display or power startup fault.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or startup behavior observed
Whether any power indicator lights were present
Whether display was blank, dim, flickering, or frozen
Any beeps, fan noise, relay clicks, or reboot loops
Whether accessories were disconnected during testing
Water trap and sample line condition
Environmental factors such as blocked vents, overheating, fluid residue, unusual odor, or physical damage

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: APNEA PRESSURE
description: Troubleshooting APNEA PRESSURE alarm conditions on a Drager Apollo anesthesia machine when breathing or ventilation has stopped, airway pressure is not detected, or fresh-gas supply, leaks, blockages, or patient connection problems may be present.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately have clinical staff ventilate the patient manually using an appropriate manual ventilation method. Remove the anesthesia machine from active patient use if ventilation cannot be quickly restored or confirmed safe. Do not continue relying on the Apollo for ventilation until the cause of the alarm is corrected.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm that the displayed alarm is APNEA PRESSURE. Ask when the alarm occurred and observe whether there is no visible chest rise, no delivered breath, no airway pressure waveform, low or flat pressure reading, or ventilator cycling without pressure change.

steps.title: Confirm Manual Ventilation and Backup Ventilation Readiness
steps.instructions: Verify that staff can manually ventilate the patient using a safe alternate method. Confirm oxygen flow is available and confirm the manual ventilation bag, APL valve setting, and breathing circuit are appropriate if the patient remains connected to the anesthesia system.

steps.title: Check Fresh-Gas Supply and Settings
steps.instructions: Check fresh-gas flow settings, increase fresh-gas flow if too low, and verify oxygen supply is available from pipeline or backup cylinder as appropriate.

steps.title: Check the Patient Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the patient connection at the airway, mask, endotracheal tube, or breathing circuit connection. Look for a disconnected Y-piece, loose elbow, loose sample line connection, or circuit pulled away from the patient.

steps.title: Inspect the Breathing Circuit for Leaks
steps.instructions: Check breathing hoses, Y-piece, breathing bag connection, absorber connections, and filter connections. Reconnect or replace any loose, cracked, misassembled, damaged, or questionable component.

steps.title: Check for Blockage or Kinking in the Tube or Hose System
steps.instructions: Inspect hoses, patient tube, airway device, and microbial filter for kinks, compression, fluid buildup, biting, occlusion, blockage, saturation, reversal, or contamination. Replace the filter if blockage or contamination is suspected.

steps.title: Check Ventilation Mode and Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm ventilation mode, tidal volume, respiratory rate, pressure settings, fresh-gas flow, and alarm limits. Verify the machine is not in setup, standby, manual/spontaneous, or another incorrect operating state when automatic ventilation is expected.

steps.title: Isolate Accessory-Related Causes
steps.instructions: Swap suspect external accessories one at a time, including the breathing circuit, Y-piece, microbial filter, sample line or airway adapter if relevant, and bag or hose assembly.

steps.title: Perform a System Check Before Returning to Use
steps.instructions: Run the appropriate machine checkout or leak/system test per facility procedure and manufacturer guidance. Confirm expected airway pressure, flow, waveform behavior, and no repeat APNEA PRESSURE alarm before returning the device to service.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine reported APNEA PRESSURE alarm during use. Staff reported ventilation stopped or airway pressure was not being detected.
CCR.Cause: Found breathing circuit disconnected at patient Y-piece, preventing pressure buildup during ventilation.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected breathing circuit, verified patient connection, checked fresh-gas flow, inspected hoses and microbial filter, ran checkout, and confirmed ventilation pressure returned with no repeat APNEA PRESSURE alarm. Device returned to service.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: APNEA FLOW
description: Troubleshooting guide for the Drager Apollo APNEA FLOW alarm when breathing or ventilation has stopped or expected ventilation flow is not being detected.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat APNEA FLOW as a patient-critical ventilation alarm. Notify anesthesia staff immediately, confirm the patient is being ventilated manually or by another appropriate backup method, and remove the machine from patient use if ventilation cannot be quickly confirmed as safe.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm is APNEA FLOW. Note whether it occurred during automatic ventilation, manual ventilation, after a circuit change, after patient connection, or after repositioning the tube or breathing circuit.

steps.title: Confirm Manual Ventilation and Patient Breathing Status
steps.instructions: Have anesthesia staff confirm whether the patient is breathing spontaneously, apneic, disconnected, or being manually ventilated. Do not let troubleshooting delay patient ventilation support.

steps.title: Check Ventilator Mode and Settings
steps.instructions: Review the active ventilation mode, respiratory rate, tidal volume or pressure setting, inspiratory pressure, trigger settings, and other parameters with anesthesia staff. Correct settings if needed and confirm the alarm clears.

steps.title: Check Fresh-Gas Supply and Fresh-Gas Settings
steps.instructions: Verify fresh-gas flow is on and set appropriately. Confirm O2 supply is available from pipeline or backup cylinder and check for related supply alarms.

steps.title: Inspect the Breathing Circuit and Hose System
steps.instructions: Check for disconnected hoses, loose fittings, damaged tubing, incorrect assembly, improperly seated bag, filter, HME, absorber, or breathing system components.

steps.title: Check for Kinked, Occluded, or Restricted Tubing
steps.instructions: Inspect the patient tube, breathing hoses, filters, and airway adapters for kinks, compression, blockage, water accumulation, or pinched tubing.

steps.title: Check for Circuit Leak
steps.instructions: Inspect common leak points including the patient Y-piece, breathing circuit connections, filter or HME connection, breathing bag connection, absorber canister, and CO2 absorber seal area. Perform leak testing only when the machine is removed from patient use.

steps.title: Check Accessories and Recently Changed Components
steps.instructions: Ask whether the breathing circuit, filter, HME, absorber canister, sample line, patient airway adapter, breathing bag, or scavenging setup was recently changed. Reinstall or replace questionable accessories.

steps.title: Isolate Whether the Issue Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: After patient safety is addressed, verify known-good circuit components, adequate fresh-gas supply, correct settings, no leaks, no kinked tubing, and proper checkout results. If the alarm persists, suspect an internal ventilation, flow sensing, breathing system, or control issue.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed APNEA FLOW alarm during use. Staff reported breathing/ventilation stopped or machine was not detecting ventilation flow.
CCR.Cause: Checked ventilator settings, fresh-gas setting, hose system, breathing circuit, patient tube routing, filter/HME, and possible leaks. Alarm appeared related to insufficient fresh-gas flow, kinked tubing, hose system leak, or unresolved ventilation/flow detection fault.
CCR.Resolution: Patient ventilation was supported by clinical staff. Fresh-gas setting, ventilator settings, breathing circuit, hose system, and tube were inspected. External issue was corrected if found. If APNEA FLOW persisted, machine was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and referred for repair evaluation.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: BATTERY LOW
description: Troubleshooting guide for a BATTERY LOW alarm on a Drager Apollo anesthesia machine when UPS battery capacity is nearly exhausted and continued powered operation may be limited.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Check the patient’s condition immediately, confirm ventilation is adequate, prepare manual ventilation with 100% O2, and have backup ventilation or another anesthesia machine available if power stability cannot be confirmed quickly.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm is BATTERY LOW. Note whether the priority is Advisory or Caution, when the alarm occurred, and whether the machine is operating on battery or mains power.

steps.title: Connect the Machine to Mains Power
steps.instructions: Plug the Apollo into a known working hospital-grade AC outlet. Confirm the power cord is fully seated and remove power strips or extension cords from the setup if allowed by facility policy.

steps.title: Verify the Outlet Has Power
steps.instructions: Check whether other devices on the same outlet are powered, test the outlet if safe and allowed, try another known working emergency-powered outlet, and confirm the outlet is not switched off or failed.

steps.title: Check the Power Cord and Connection Points
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord for damage, loose fit, bent prongs, cuts, crushing, overheating, or strain. If approved and available, swap with a known-good detachable cord.

steps.title: Check for Facility Power or Circuit Issues
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff about recent power outages, room power issues, tripped breakers, or outlet failures. Escalate suspected outlet or circuit issues to Facilities or Plant Operations.

steps.title: Confirm Battery Charging Behavior
steps.instructions: Once connected to mains power, observe whether AC power is recognized and battery charging begins. Confirm the battery level stabilizes or recovers and the alarm clears after sufficient charging.

steps.title: Isolate External Power Issue vs Device Issue
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after moving outlets or changing the cord, suspect the original power source or cord. If the alarm persists with known-good mains power and secure cord, suspect an internal battery, charging, or power supply issue.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If verified mains power clears the alarm and charging is normal, document the checks performed, confirm stable operation, allow recharge per department practice, and return the device to service only if no related power alarms remain.

CCR.Complaint: Apollo anesthesia machine displayed BATTERY LOW alarm. Staff reported unit was operating with low UPS battery capacity.
CCR.Cause: Machine was found operating on battery power due to loss of reliable mains power. Outlet, power cord, and AC connection were checked.
CCR.Resolution: Connected unit to known-good mains power, verified AC power recognition, monitored battery charging behavior, and confirmed BATTERY LOW alarm cleared. If alarm persisted, unit was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for repair evaluation.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: BREATH. SYS. TEMP. HIGH
description: Troubleshooting guide for the Drager Apollo BREATH. SYS. TEMP. HIGH alarm when breathing system temperature is too high and may affect delivered gas safety or machine operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the anesthesia machine while relying on it for active patient ventilation unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. Remove the machine from patient use if the alarm is active, repeated, or associated with unusual heat, odor, abnormal ventilation, or patient circuit concerns.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is BREATH. SYS. TEMP. HIGH. Note whether it occurs during startup, checkout, ventilation, extended use, or intermittently. Check for warm components, warm hoses, unusual odor, ventilation interruption, or additional alarms.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Heat Before Continuing
steps.instructions: Carefully check the outside of the breathing system, absorber area, patient circuit connection points, and nearby surfaces for unusual heat. Do not open internal covers or disassemble the breathing system beyond normal user-removable components.

steps.title: Check Breathing Gas and External Heat Conditions
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff whether delivered gas or circuit temperature seemed unusually warm. Check for heated humidification, external warming accessories, forced-air warming devices, or other heat sources near the anesthesia circuit.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Breathing Circuit
steps.instructions: Check breathing hoses, filters, HME, Y-piece, and circuit connections for kinks, restrictions, blocked filters, saturated filters, contaminated parts, incorrect installation, or components positioned near heat sources.

steps.title: Check the Breathing System Assembly
steps.instructions: Verify the breathing system is properly seated and assembled. Inspect the absorber canister, visible seals, gaskets, accessible components, and connection points for loose parts, damage, contamination, fluid, or abnormal heat.

steps.title: Check Work Area and Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the anesthesia machine is not near a heat source. Check for blocked vents, supplies covering ventilation openings, direct sunlight, hot room conditions, or equipment positioned too close to the machine.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only After Patient Safety Is Addressed
steps.instructions: After the device is removed from patient use, turn the machine off and allow it to cool if warm. Restart and observe whether the alarm returns during startup or checkout.

steps.title: Perform a Full Pre-Use / Checkout Test
steps.instructions: After any correction, complete the required checkout. Confirm no BREATH. SYS. TEMP. HIGH alarm, no additional breathing system alarms, normal ventilation, acceptable leak/system checks, and normal gas flow behavior.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after correcting circuit setup, blocked airflow, external heat, or accessory issues, the cause was likely external. If it returns with proper setup and known-good accessories, remove the device from service and escalate for repair.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed BREATH. SYS. TEMP. HIGH alarm during use / checkout. Staff reported breathing system temperature alarm and concern for elevated breathing system temperature.
CCR.Cause: Checked breathing circuit, filters, absorber area, external heat sources, ventilation openings, and breathing system setup. No external cause found. Alarm indicates breathing system temperature is too high; likely internal temperature sensing, heating, control, or breathing system fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed machine from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated for repair evaluation / DragerService support. Device not returned to service due to unresolved BREATH. SYS. TEMP. HIGH alarm.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: CHECK AUX CGO
description: Troubleshooting CHECK AUX CGO when fresh-gas delivery or switching between Aux CGO and ventilation modes may be unreliable.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot Aux CGO switching problems during active patient care unless the anesthesia provider confirms it is safe. Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if fresh-gas delivery, manual ventilation, or mode switching is questionable. Use an alternate anesthesia machine, manual ventilation method, or approved backup gas delivery method as directed by anesthesia staff.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is CHECK AUX CGO. Determine whether the alarm occurs during switch-over to Aux CGO, when switching back from Aux CGO, during setup, or during checkout. Ask staff whether the bag failed to inflate, fresh gas was not present, or the machine would not return properly from Aux CGO.

steps.title: Check Fresh-Gas Flow at the Aux CGO
steps.instructions: With the machine out of patient use, verify that fresh-gas flow is present at the Aux CGO when Aux CGO is selected. Confirm that flow settings are appropriate and observe whether a connected test bag inflates when fresh gas should be delivered.

steps.title: Switch Aux CGO On and Off Several Times
steps.instructions: Select Aux CGO on and off several times while observing alarm behavior and fresh-gas delivery. Allow the machine to complete each switching action before changing modes again. Confirm that switching becomes normal and the alarm does not return.

steps.title: Check External and Simple Causes
steps.instructions: Inspect for loose or damaged bag connections, disconnected tubing, kinked tubing, obstructed tubing, incorrect setup, incorrect mode selection, physical damage, contamination, or liquid intrusion around the Aux CGO area.

steps.title: Remove From Service if Unresolved
steps.instructions: If CHECK AUX CGO continues after fresh-gas flow, setup, tubing, outlet use, and Aux CGO switching have been checked, remove the anesthesia machine from service, label it Out of Service, and send it for repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed CHECK AUX CGO alarm. Staff reported fault when switching to auxiliary common gas outlet and/or switching back from Aux CGO to another ventilation mode.
CCR.Cause: Verified CHECK AUX CGO alarm. Fresh-gas flow and Aux CGO switching were checked. External setup, tubing, bag connection, and outlet issues were inspected. Persistent alarm indicates likely internal Aux CGO switching or sensing fault.
CCR.Resolution: Machine removed from service and labeled Out of Service. Aux CGO not considered reliable for clinical use. Referred for repair or DragerService evaluation.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: CIRCUIT LEAK
description: Troubleshooting CIRCUIT LEAK alarms on a Drager Apollo anesthesia machine when a leak is detected in the patient circle system, breathing circuit, hose, tube, filter, bag, absorber, or adapter connection.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Notify anesthesia staff, assess ventilation and oxygenation, and do not continue relying on the anesthesia machine if ventilation is unstable or the leak cannot be quickly corrected.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is CIRCUIT LEAK and note when it appears, including during checkout, manual ventilation, mechanical ventilation, circuit change, or patient connection.

steps.title: Check the Patient Breathing Circuit
steps.instructions: Inspect inspiratory and expiratory circuit limbs, hose connections, Y-piece, elbows, adapters, and tubing for loose connections, cracks, or disconnections.

steps.title: Check the Tube or Airway Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the endotracheal tube, LMA, elbow, sample line adapter, test lung, breathing bag connection, and patient-side connection for leakage or poor seal.

steps.title: Check the Breathing Filter or HME
steps.instructions: Verify the filter or HME is properly seated and not cracked, loose, wet, obstructed, or connected through a poor adapter stack.

steps.title: Check Adapters and Accessories
steps.instructions: Inspect CO2 sampling adapters, inline suction adapters, nebulizer adapters, extensions, elbows, dead-space adapters, and test lung adapters for leaks.

steps.title: Check the Breathing Bag and Manual Ventilation Path
steps.instructions: Inspect the breathing bag, bag arm connection, APL valve setup, and manual ventilation path for obvious leakage or poor seating.

steps.title: Check the CO2 Absorber Canister and Breathing System Seating
steps.instructions: Verify the absorber canister is locked in place, seals are clean and undamaged, and no debris or granules are preventing a proper seal.

steps.title: Perform a Leak Check or Checkout Test
steps.instructions: After correcting external issues, perform the appropriate anesthesia machine checkout or leak test and confirm the alarm clears before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Remove From Service if the Problem Persists
steps.instructions: If the alarm continues after external circuit components are checked or replaced, label the anesthesia machine Out of Service and send it for repair or qualified bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine reported CIRCUIT LEAK alarm. Staff reported leak in patient circle system during setup/use with concern for breathing circuit leak.
CCR.Cause: Leak traced to loose/damaged external circuit component, breathing hose, tube connection, filter, breathing bag, absorber seal, or adapter connection.
CCR.Resolution: Checked patient breathing circuit, tube connection, hoses, filter, adapters, breathing bag, and absorber canister. Corrected/replaced leaking component and performed leak check. Alarm cleared and unit passed checkout. Returned to service.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: CO2 SENSOR FAIL CO2
description: Troubleshooting CO2 SENSOR FAIL CO2 on a Drager Apollo anesthesia machine when the onboard CO2 gas measurement system has failed or CO2 monitoring is unavailable.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot CO2 monitoring failures on an active patient unless clinical staff have secured safe ventilation and alternate monitoring. Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if CO2 monitoring is unavailable and no approved alternate monitoring is in place.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is CO2 SENSOR FAIL CO2. Check whether the alarm appears in Standby, during startup, after sitting unused, or after restart. Observe whether CO2 readings or other gas monitoring values are missing or unstable.

steps.title: Confirm the Machine Is Not Being Relied On for CO2 Monitoring
steps.instructions: Check whether the Apollo is connected to a patient circuit, breathing bag, test lung, or clinical setup. Confirm with anesthesia staff whether the machine's internal CO2 monitoring is being relied on.

steps.title: Check for Other Related Gas Monitoring Alarms
steps.instructions: Look for additional alarms related to gas measurement, sampling, or monitoring failure. Check whether O2, anesthetic agent, or other gas values are also affected.

steps.title: Check External and Simple Causes First
steps.instructions: Inspect the visible external gas monitoring setup if applicable. Check for loose, missing, blocked, kinked, or contaminated sampling tubing, moisture, fluid contamination, damaged water trap, or improperly seated accessories.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only If Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the device is not connected to a patient and clinical staff approve, power the Apollo down and restart it. Allow startup checks to complete and confirm whether the alarm returns.

steps.title: Verify External CO2 Monitoring If the Unit Must Remain Clinically Available
steps.instructions: If the Apollo is needed but internal CO2 monitoring is unavailable, confirm with clinical leadership whether an approved external gas measuring system will be used. Do not independently approve clinical use without reliable monitoring.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Accessory, Configuration, or Device Related
steps.instructions: If an external setup issue was found and corrected, retest the machine. If the alarm returns with proper setup and after restart, treat it as a device-related CO2 gas measurement failure.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears, verify CO2 monitoring returns to normal, confirm no related gas measurement alarms are present, perform an operational checkout, return the device to service only after normal function is confirmed, and document the corrective action.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed CO2 SENSOR FAIL CO2 alarm in Standby. Staff reported onboard CO2 gas measurement was unavailable.
CCR.Cause: Confirmed CO2 SENSOR FAIL CO2 alarm. External sampling setup, visible connections, sample line, and water trap were checked with no correctable external issue found. Alarm persisted after safe restart, indicating likely internal CO2 gas measurement system failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed anesthesia machine from service and labeled Out of Service. Advised use of approved external gas measuring system if clinically required. Referred unit for DragerService or qualified bench evaluation.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: CONTINUOUS PRESSURE
description: Troubleshooting guide for the Drager Apollo CONTINUOUS PRESSURE alarm when breathing pressure exceeds the set limit for more than 15 seconds.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is being ventilated safely, notify anesthesia staff immediately, prepare alternate ventilation if needed, and do not continue relying on the anesthesia machine if pressure remains elevated or ventilation appears unsafe.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is CONTINUOUS PRESSURE and identify when it occurs, such as during mechanical ventilation, manual/spontaneous ventilation, patient connection, test lung use, or intermittently during certain cases.

steps.title: Remove From Patient Use if Pressure Does Not Clear
steps.instructions: If pressure does not return to normal, have clinical staff ventilate the patient by an alternate safe method and remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if safe ventilation cannot be confirmed.

steps.title: Check Ventilation and Patient Breathing Conditions
steps.instructions: Have anesthesia staff assess ventilation and spontaneous breathing, including coughing, bearing down, obstruction, or breathing against the ventilator.

steps.title: Check the Breathing Circuit and Hoses
steps.instructions: Inspect hoses, filters, HME, connectors, and circuit routing for kinks, crushed tubing, water accumulation, blockage, improper seating, or accessory restriction.

steps.title: Check the Breathing System Assembly
steps.instructions: Verify the breathing system, breathing bag, absorber, valves, and visible components are properly installed, fully seated, and not visibly blocked or damaged.

steps.title: Check the Gas Scavenging System
steps.instructions: Inspect the scavenging tubing, wall connection, and scavenging interface for kinks, blockage, improper connection, or restriction that could create back pressure.

steps.title: Check Alarm Limit Settings
steps.instructions: Have anesthesia staff verify the pressure alarm limit is appropriate for the patient, ventilation mode, and clinical situation. Do not raise limits simply to silence the alarm.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Breathing Circuit or Test Lung
steps.instructions: After patient care is no longer dependent on the unit, connect a known-good circuit and test lung, run ventilation with appropriate settings, and observe whether the alarm returns.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: When the alarm clears, confirm normal ventilation, expected pressure readings, no additional alarms, and complete the required checkout before returning the anesthesia machine to service.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine reported CONTINUOUS PRESSURE alarm. Staff stated breathing pressure exceeded the alarm limit during ventilation and the alarm did not clear normally.
CCR.Cause: Breathing circuit inspection found a kinked expiratory hose causing restricted exhalation and sustained breathing pressure above the set alarm limit.
CCR.Resolution: Removed kinked circuit tubing, installed proper breathing circuit setup, verified ventilation with test lung, confirmed pressure returned to expected range, and confirmed CONTINUOUS PRESSURE alarm did not return. Unit returned to service.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: FAN FAIL
description: Troubleshooting FAN FAIL on a Drager Apollo anesthesia machine when internal gas evacuation may be impaired, creating possible elevated oxygen concentration and fire risk.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat FAN FAIL as a serious safety condition. If the machine is in active use, notify anesthesia staff immediately and support transition to another anesthesia machine or safe manual ventilation method. Do not continue using the machine longer than clinically necessary.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is FAN FAIL. Ask when the alarm appeared and document whether it occurred at startup, during a case, after extended runtime, intermittently, or continuously.

steps.title: Do Not Attempt Continued Operation
steps.instructions: Do not silence the alarm and continue routine clinical use as a workaround. Do not rely on the anesthesia machine until the cause has been evaluated.

steps.title: Power Down the Machine Safely
steps.instructions: Once the patient is safely off the machine and clinical staff confirm it is safe, switch the anesthesia machine off as quickly as possible using the site’s normal shutdown process unless urgent power removal is required.

steps.title: Check for Obvious External Conditions Only
steps.instructions: Perform a limited external inspection without opening the machine. Check for blocked vents, items stored against the machine, contamination near intake or exhaust areas, liquid spill, unusual smell, unusual heat, visible damage, recent movement, impact, or recent service activity.

steps.title: Verify Power Source and Environment
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine was connected to appropriate facility power. Check whether the alarm occurred during a power issue, room power interruption, or after moving the machine. Inspect the power cord and plug for obvious damage.

steps.title: Do Not Open the Machine for Internal Fan Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: Do not remove covers, bypass fan circuits, operate the machine with covers removed, or defeat the alarm. Escalate internal fan or ventilation system concerns to DragerService or qualified anesthesia service support.

steps.title: Remove From Service
steps.instructions: Label the anesthesia machine Out of Service, include FAN FAIL on the tag, move it away from clinical use if possible, and notify the appropriate service pathway or vendor support.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed FAN FAIL alarm. Staff reported concern that the fan for evacuating gases inside the device may be defective.
CCR.Cause: Confirmed FAN FAIL alarm. External vents, power source, and visible machine condition were checked. No safe external correction was available. Alarm indicates possible internal fan or internal ventilation system fault requiring service evaluation.
CCR.Resolution: Removed anesthesia machine from patient use, powered unit down after safe clinical transition, labeled Out of Service, and escalated for DragerService or qualified repair evaluation.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: FG LOW OR LEAK
description: Troubleshooting guide for the FG LOW OR LEAK alarm on a Drager Apollo anesthesia machine when fresh-gas flow is too low or a leak prevents proper gas delivery.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Have anesthesia staff assess the patient immediately, confirm ventilation and oxygenation are safe, and use an alternate approved ventilation method if the alarm cannot be corrected quickly.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is FG LOW OR LEAK and determine whether it occurs during startup, checkout, patient connection, ventilation, fresh-gas setting changes, or circuit changes.

steps.title: Increase Fresh-Gas Flow
steps.instructions: Have qualified anesthesia staff increase the fresh-gas flow setting and observe whether the alarm clears.

steps.title: Check the Breathing Circuit for Leaks
steps.instructions: Inspect the patient circuit, Y-piece, breathing bag, absorber area, sample line, water trap connection, and hose connections for loose, cracked, disconnected, or damaged components.

steps.title: Check the CO2 Absorber and Canister Seal
steps.instructions: Confirm the absorber canister is properly seated and latched, and check for damaged seals, cracked housing, or improper installation.

steps.title: Check the Breathing Bag and Manual Ventilation Path
steps.instructions: Inspect the breathing bag, bag hose, bag arm, and APL valve position for leaks or incorrect setup.

steps.title: Check the Anesthetic Gas Receiving System AGS
steps.instructions: Inspect the AGS and scavenging setup for disconnected, kinked, blocked, or incorrectly connected tubing.

steps.title: Run a Leak Check or Machine Checkout
steps.instructions: Remove the machine from patient use and perform the approved checkout or leak test to identify whether the issue is external or internal.

steps.title: Isolate External Accessories
steps.instructions: Replace suspect external components one at a time, including the breathing circuit, breathing bag, sample line, water trap, absorber canister, and AGS tubing.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears, confirm it does not return, verify the machine passes checkout, return it to service only after normal operation is confirmed, and document the final status.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed FG LOW OR LEAK alarm during use. Staff reported concern for fresh-gas shortage or breathing system leak.
CCR.Cause: Fresh-gas flow setting was low for current use, or leak was suspected in the breathing circuit, absorber, breathing bag, AGS, or internal gas path. External checks were performed to isolate the cause.
CCR.Resolution: Fresh-gas flow was increased, breathing circuit and absorber canister were inspected or reseated, AGS connection was verified, and machine checkout was completed. If the alarm cleared and checkout passed, the unit was returned to service. If the alarm persisted, the unit was removed from service for repair evaluation.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: O2 PIPELINE FAIL
description: Troubleshooting guide for the O2 PIPELINE FAIL alarm on the Drager Apollo anesthesia machine when oxygen supply is lost, restricted, disconnected, or unavailable.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Notify anesthesia staff immediately if the alarm occurs during patient use. Confirm adequate ventilation and oxygen delivery. Use an alternate oxygen source, manual ventilation method, or backup anesthesia machine if oxygen delivery is unstable.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm is O2 PIPELINE FAIL. Note whether it occurs at startup, setup, active use, after moving the machine, or after gas hose or cylinder changes. Check for low O2 pressure, reduced flow, disconnected hoses, low cylinder pressure, leaks, or kinked tubing.

steps.title: Open the O2 Backup Cylinder
steps.instructions: Check that the O2 backup cylinder is installed, open the cylinder valve, and verify cylinder pressure. If the alarm clears, continue checking the pipeline source before returning the machine to normal use.

steps.title: Check the O2 Pipeline Hose Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the oxygen pipeline hose from the Apollo to the wall outlet. Confirm the hose is fully connected at both the machine and wall outlet. Look for loose fittings, damaged connectors, or partially seated quick-connect fittings.

steps.title: Check for a Kinked, Pinched, or Damaged O2 Hose
steps.instructions: Trace the full length of the oxygen hose. Look for kinks, sharp bends, crushed sections, visible damage, leaks, or hose trapped under wheels or equipment. Reposition the hose if needed.

steps.title: Check the Wall O2 Pipeline Supply
steps.instructions: Confirm the wall outlet is the correct oxygen outlet. Check whether other nearby oxygen outlets are functioning. Ask clinical staff or Facilities about possible medical gas issues and try a known-good outlet if safe.

steps.title: Check the O2 Cylinder Status
steps.instructions: Verify the O2 cylinder is not empty, the valve is open, and the cylinder is properly installed. Replace the cylinder with a full cylinder if pressure is low or empty.

steps.title: Compare Pipeline vs Cylinder Operation
steps.instructions: With clinical approval and only when safe, compare operation on pipeline oxygen and backup cylinder oxygen. Use the results to separate a pipeline source issue, cylinder issue, or possible internal machine issue.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious External Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the gas inlet area, pipeline hose, cylinder connection area, and visible fittings for broken connectors, missing seals, damaged hose ends, impact damage, or unusual hissing.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after correcting the oxygen source issue, verify stable oxygen supply, confirm the alarm does not return, confirm anesthesia staff are comfortable with the final status, and document the correction.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed O2 PIPELINE FAIL alarm during setup/use. Staff reported possible loss of oxygen supply.
CCR.Cause: Found O2 pipeline hose disconnected/kinked, wall oxygen supply unavailable, or O2 backup cylinder empty/closed. External oxygen supply checks were completed.
CCR.Resolution: Opened O2 backup cylinder, checked pipeline hose connection, inspected hose for restriction or damage, verified wall pipeline supply, and confirmed cylinder pressure/valve status. Alarm cleared after correcting oxygen supply issue, or machine was removed from service for further evaluation.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: O2 CYLIND. EMPTY
description: Troubleshooting guide for the Drager Apollo O2 CYLIND. EMPTY alarm when the O2 backup cylinder is empty or closed and pipeline oxygen supply is unavailable, missing, or not connected.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately notify anesthesia staff. Do not continue using the machine for patient care if oxygen supply is not available or cannot be confirmed. Move the patient to a safe oxygen source, manual ventilation method, or alternate anesthesia system as clinically appropriate.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is exactly O2 CYLIND. EMPTY. Observe whether the alarm occurs at startup, during pre-use checkout, during case setup, during active use, or after pipeline oxygen is disconnected. Check for related signs such as low O2 supply pressure, missing pipeline oxygen, closed cylinder valve, empty cylinder, failed checkout, or oxygen supply pressure alarms.

steps.title: Check Pipeline Oxygen Supply First
steps.instructions: Verify the wall oxygen hose is connected to the proper oxygen outlet and to the Apollo O2 pipeline inlet. Confirm the hose is fully seated and inspect it for damage, kinks, incorrect gas connection, missing fittings, or loose connections.

steps.title: Confirm Wall Oxygen Source Is Available
steps.instructions: Verify that the wall oxygen outlet is supplying oxygen. Try a known-good oxygen outlet if available and clinically appropriate. If there is concern about facility oxygen supply, notify Facilities, Respiratory Therapy, or the appropriate clinical support team immediately.

steps.title: Check the O2 Backup Cylinder Valve
steps.instructions: Verify an O2 cylinder is installed. Confirm the cylinder valve is open and the cylinder is properly seated and secured. If opening the cylinder valve clears the alarm, complete the required anesthesia machine checkout before return to clinical use.

steps.title: Check O2 Cylinder Pressure
steps.instructions: Read the O2 cylinder pressure gauge. Confirm the cylinder is not empty or below usable pressure. Replace the O2 cylinder if the pressure is empty, very low, or questionable.

steps.title: Inspect Cylinder Installation
steps.instructions: Confirm the cylinder is installed correctly in the yoke or cylinder connection. Check for misalignment, missing or damaged washer or seal, loose connection, or obvious leak.

steps.title: Isolate Pipeline Versus Cylinder Issue
steps.instructions: With clinical approval and only when safe, check whether the alarm clears with a known-good pipeline oxygen source connected or a known-good O2 cylinder installed and opened. If neither supply source clears the alarm, the issue may be internal to the machine.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after correcting the pipeline oxygen connection, opening the O2 cylinder, or replacing the cylinder, run the required anesthesia machine checkout. Confirm no oxygen supply alarms return and return the machine to service only after checkout passes.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed O2 CYLIND. EMPTY alarm. Staff reported O2 backup cylinder was unavailable and pipeline O2 supply was not connected or not available.
CCR.Cause: O2 backup cylinder was empty or closed while pipeline oxygen supply was unavailable. External oxygen supply issue confirmed before internal failure was suspected.
CCR.Resolution: Verified patient safety and removed machine from use during troubleshooting. Checked pipeline O2 connection, wall source, O2 cylinder valve, cylinder pressure, and cylinder installation. Opened or replaced O2 cylinder and restored pipeline oxygen supply as needed. Alarm cleared and anesthesia machine checkout passed before return to service.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: O2 FLOW MEAS. FAIL
description: Troubleshooting guide for the Drager Apollo O2 FLOW MEAS. FAIL alarm when the anesthesia machine cannot reliably measure oxygen fresh-gas flow.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the machine is in active patient use. If oxygen flow measurement is unreliable, have anesthesia staff verify ventilation and oxygen delivery using approved clinical monitoring or move to a backup anesthesia machine or safe ventilation method.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm is O2 FLOW MEAS. FAIL. Note when it occurs, what staff observed, whether O2 flow readings are incorrect or missing, and whether other fresh-gas, oxygen, ventilator, or self-test alarms are active.

steps.title: Remove the Machine From Patient Use if Active and Unresolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm is present during checkout, setup, or between cases, do not release the Apollo for patient use. If it occurs during use, support anesthesia staff in moving to another machine if they determine the alarm cannot be safely managed.

steps.title: Use Oxygen Only as Fresh Gas for Initial Isolation
steps.instructions: When safe to test, set the machine to use oxygen as the only fresh gas. Observe the total flow meter while adjusting O2 fresh-gas flow to determine whether total flow responds logically.

steps.title: Compare Fresh-Gas Flow Settings to the Total Flow Meter
steps.instructions: Check the set O2 fresh-gas flow against the total flow meter. Look for mismatch, no response, unstable response, or flow behavior that does not match the control setting.

steps.title: Confirm Fresh-Gas Flow Is Adequate for Ventilation Needs
steps.instructions: Use the total flow meter to confirm fresh-gas flow. Under clinical direction, confirm fresh-gas flow is greater than or equal to the measured or set minute volume when applicable.

steps.title: Check External Gas Supply Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the oxygen pipeline hose is connected, the wall outlet is active, backup cylinder pressure is adequate if applicable, and the oxygen hose is not kinked, damaged, loose, or incorrectly connected.

steps.title: Check for Setup or Configuration Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine is configured for the intended gas setup. Check whether the alarm followed a gas supply change, machine move, circuit change, checkout failure, or recent service.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only if Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the machine is not connected to a patient, restart it according to facility procedure and allow startup checks to complete. Confirm whether O2 FLOW MEAS. FAIL clears or returns.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is External, Configuration-Related, or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If oxygen supply is stable, total flow responds logically, setup checks are normal, and the alarm remains active or returns, suspect an internal O2 fresh-gas flow measurement fault.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after correcting an external supply, setup, or flow setting issue, run the appropriate pre-use checkout, verify total flow response, and return the machine to service only after required checks pass.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine reported O2 FLOW MEAS. FAIL during setup. Staff reported the O2 fresh-gas flow measurement was not reliable.
CCR.Cause: Checked oxygen pipeline connection, oxygen supply hose, flow settings, and total flow meter response. Alarm persisted after external checks, indicating likely internal O2 fresh-gas flow measurement fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed Apollo anesthesia machine from service and labeled Out of Service. Advised staff not to use for patient care. Unit requires bench evaluation and Drager service support for persistent O2 FLOW MEAS. FAIL alarm.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: O2 SENSOR FAIL
description: Troubleshooting O2 SENSOR FAIL on a Drager Apollo anesthesia machine when oxygen concentration monitoring is unavailable or unreliable due to a defective O2 sensor or related oxygen monitoring fault.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if oxygen monitoring is unavailable, unreliable, or not backed up by an approved substitute monitor. Use adequate substitute oxygen monitoring only under anesthesia provider direction. Do not rely on the Apollo displayed oxygen concentration while O2 SENSOR FAIL is active.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is O2 SENSOR FAIL. Note whether it occurs during startup, system check, case setup, active use, or intermittently. Check whether the oxygen reading is missing, frozen, inaccurate, replaced by dashes, or shown with an alarm condition.

steps.title: Confirm Adequate Substitute Monitoring
steps.instructions: Verify that an independent oxygen analyzer or approved substitute oxygen monitor is available and functioning if the machine is being used temporarily. Confirm it is positioned to measure delivered oxygen concentration appropriately.

steps.title: Check External Gas Supply and Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm oxygen pipeline pressure is present and stable. Confirm oxygen cylinder backup is available if required by facility policy. Check that gas supply hoses are connected correctly and are not damaged, kinked, loose, or incorrectly connected.

steps.title: Check for Recent Setup, Service, or Sensor Handling
steps.instructions: Ask whether the alarm appeared after cleaning, circuit setup, sensor replacement, service activity, system check failure, or machine movement. Inspect accessible external areas for obvious damage, contamination, moisture, or loose connections.

steps.title: Restart and Recheck Only If Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: Only restart the Apollo if the machine is not connected to a patient and it is safe to do so. Power cycle the unit according to normal facility practice, allow startup and system checks to complete, and observe whether O2 SENSOR FAIL clears or returns.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Repeated Bedside Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: If O2 SENSOR FAIL returns after basic external checks, stop troubleshooting at the bedside. Do not bypass the alarm, assume the oxygen reading is acceptable, or continue extended use without dependable oxygen concentration monitoring.

steps.title: Escalate for Repair
steps.instructions: Remove the Apollo from service, label it Out of Service, notify appropriate clinical leadership, and send the unit for bench evaluation or DragerService repair. Do not return it to patient use until oxygen monitoring has been repaired and verified.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed O2 SENSOR FAIL alarm. Staff reported oxygen monitoring was unavailable or unreliable during setup/use.
CCR.Cause: Confirmed O2 SENSOR FAIL alarm. External gas supply and basic setup checks did not correct issue. Alarm information indicates defective O2 sensor or related oxygen monitoring fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed anesthesia machine from service and labeled Out of Service. Verified need for adequate substitute oxygen monitoring. Referred unit for bench evaluation/DragerService repair before return to patient use.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: PEEP HIGH
description: Troubleshooting guide for Drager Apollo PEEP HIGH alarms caused by elevated expiratory pressure, ventilation settings, breathing circuit restriction, scavenging restriction, patient airway issues, or device-related pressure control problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is being adequately ventilated and monitored. Have anesthesia staff assess airway, chest movement, oxygenation, ventilation, and circuit pressures. If pressure remains elevated or ventilation is questionable, switch to manual ventilation or another anesthesia machine as clinically appropriate.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is PEEP HIGH. Observe whether it occurs during automatic ventilation, Pressure Support mode, after two breaths, after prolonged elevated expiratory pressure, or intermittently during the case.

steps.title: Check Ventilation Parameters
steps.instructions: Review ventilation mode, set PEEP, pressure limit, inspiratory pressure, pressure support level, respiratory rate, inspiratory time, I:E ratio, tidal volume settings, and fresh gas flow with anesthesia staff.

steps.title: Check for Patient or Airway Causes
steps.instructions: Have clinical staff evaluate for airway obstruction, bronchospasm, secretions, kinked or obstructed endotracheal tube, coughing, patient-ventilator fighting, or reduced lung compliance.

steps.title: Inspect the Breathing Circuit
steps.instructions: Check for kinked tubing, occluded expiratory limb, water accumulation, blocked filters, incorrectly installed HME or bacterial filter, obstructed Y-piece, or misconnected circuit tubing.

steps.title: Check the Expiratory Valve Area and Manual/Automatic Ventilation Setup
steps.instructions: Verify the breathing system is assembled correctly, manual/automatic ventilation selection is correct, and the APL valve is not affecting ventilation when automatic ventilation is expected. Inspect accessible expiratory valve areas for visible contamination, sticking, moisture, or obstruction.

steps.title: Check the Anesthetic Gas Scavenging Line
steps.instructions: Inspect the scavenging line for kinks, pinched tubing, blocked tubing, improper connection, excessive back pressure, full or restricted waste gas interface, or incorrect scavenging setup.

steps.title: Check External Accessories and Filters
steps.instructions: Replace questionable external accessories one at a time, including the breathing circuit, expiratory filter, HME/filter, sampling line if affecting setup, scavenging hose, and reservoir bag if relevant.

steps.title: Run a Pre-Use or System Check
steps.instructions: Remove the machine from patient use and run the Apollo pre-use or system check according to facility procedure. Verify leak, compliance, valve, ventilation, and breathing system checks pass as applicable.

steps.title: Isolate Whether the Issue Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: After the patient is no longer connected, test with known-good breathing circuit, filters, correct ventilation settings, clear scavenging pathway, and proper breathing system assembly. Observe whether the PEEP HIGH alarm returns.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed PEEP HIGH alarm during automatic ventilation. Expiratory pressure remained above the set PEEP level during use.
CCR.Cause: Ventilation parameters and breathing circuit were checked. Scavenging line was inspected and found restricted, creating excess pressure during exhalation.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected scavenging line routing and verified proper connection. Replaced questionable external tubing as needed. Ran machine check and confirmed PEEP HIGH alarm did not return. Device returned to service.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: PINSP NOT ACHIEVED
description: Troubleshooting guide for the Drager Apollo PINSP NOT ACHIEVED alarm when set inspiratory pressure is not reached due to leak, ventilation settings, accessory issues, or fresh-gas shortage.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is being ventilated adequately. Notify the anesthesia provider immediately if the alarm occurs during active patient use. If ventilation is not reliable, have clinical staff manually ventilate the patient or transfer ventilation to another safe method. Do not continue relying on the machine if inspiratory pressure cannot be achieved.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is PINSP NOT ACHIEVED. Verify the machine is operating in Pressure Mode or another pressure-controlled ventilation mode. Observe when the alarm occurs and check for low tidal volume, low airway pressure, audible leak, abnormal ventilator behavior, low fresh-gas flow, or inability to maintain set pressure.

steps.title: Check the Set Ventilation Parameters
steps.instructions: Review the pressure control settings with the anesthesia provider. Check set inspiratory pressure, PEEP, respiratory rate, inspiratory time, I:E ratio, fresh-gas flow, ventilation mode, patient size, and circuit type. Correct inappropriate settings if found and verify stable ventilation.

steps.title: Check for Breathing Circuit Leaks
steps.instructions: Inspect the breathing circuit from the machine to the patient connection. Check tubing, Y-piece, patient elbow, filter or HME, sample line, breathing bag, APL valve position if relevant, absorber canister seating, water trap, loose fittings, and damaged tubing.

steps.title: Check the Patient Connection and Airway Interface
steps.instructions: Have clinical staff verify the patient airway connection. Check for loose endotracheal tube connection, leaking cuff, disconnected airway adapter, poor mask seal, loose supraglottic airway connection, or patient movement affecting the seal.

steps.title: Check Fresh-Gas Flow and Gas Supply
steps.instructions: Review fresh-gas setting and gas supply condition. Confirm fresh-gas flow is appropriate, oxygen and air pipeline supplies are connected, backup cylinder pressure is adequate if used, no gas supply alarm is active, and flow controls respond normally.

steps.title: Perform a Leak Test or System Check
steps.instructions: When safe and not connected to a patient, run the appropriate machine checkout, leak test, or system test. Confirm whether the breathing system passes leak testing. Keep the machine out of patient use if it fails leak testing and no external leak source is corrected.

steps.title: Isolate Accessory-Related Causes
steps.instructions: Swap or remove external accessories one at a time if safe to do so. Check the breathing circuit, HME/filter, water trap, CO2 sample line, breathing bag, absorber canister, and patient airway adapter.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage, Contamination, or Misassembly
steps.instructions: Inspect for cracked fittings, damaged seals, absorber not latched, missing or damaged canister gasket, foreign material on sealing surfaces, liquid contamination, loose external panels, or loose ports.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: After checking settings, circuit connections, accessories, fresh-gas flow, and leak test results, determine whether the issue is likely setup-related or device-related. Escalate if the alarm persists with known-good external components and adequate gas supply.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo displayed PINSP NOT ACHIEVED alarm during Pressure Mode ventilation. Set inspiratory pressure was not being reached.
CCR.Cause: Breathing circuit leak found at loose patient filter connection. Fresh-gas supply and ventilation settings were checked and no gas supply issue was found.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated filter and circuit connection. Verified alarm cleared, ventilation pressure stabilized, and machine passed checkout. Returned to service.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: POWER FAIL
description: Troubleshooting POWER FAIL alarm conditions on a Drager Apollo anesthesia machine caused by AC power loss, battery operation, or a possible auxiliary outlet short-circuit.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is being safely ventilated. Notify anesthesia staff immediately if the machine is in active use. Prepare manual ventilation if ventilation support is affected or if power cannot be restored quickly. Do not rely on the machine if power status is unstable or battery capacity is low.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is POWER FAIL. Note whether it occurs during startup, active use, after plugging in an accessory, after moving the machine, or after connecting to a different outlet. Check for battery operation, low battery indication, auxiliary outlet power loss, accessory failure, ventilator instability, or display instability.

steps.title: Check Main AC Power First
steps.instructions: Verify the Apollo power cord is fully connected to the machine and wall outlet. Check whether the wall outlet has power. Test the outlet according to facility policy or try a known-good hospital-grade outlet if the original outlet is questionable.

steps.title: Observe Battery Status
steps.instructions: Check battery capacity or battery status indication. Confirm whether the machine is running on battery backup. If AC power is restored, verify the machine recognizes AC power and begins normal charging behavior.

steps.title: Check for Auxiliary Outlet Overload or Short-Circuit
steps.instructions: Identify devices plugged into the Apollo auxiliary outlets. Unplug connected auxiliary outlet devices one at a time and observe whether the POWER FAIL alarm clears. Inspect accessory cords and plugs for damage, bent prongs, fluid contamination, overheating, or burning smell. Remove any suspect accessory from service.

steps.title: Verify Pipeline Supply and Machine Readiness
steps.instructions: Confirm required gas pipeline connections are secure and supplying appropriate pressure. Check that oxygen and air pipeline hoses are connected correctly. Verify backup gas cylinders are available and have adequate pressure according to facility practice.

steps.title: Check External Power Cord and Physical Condition
