steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord, plug, machine power inlet, auxiliary outlets, and rear panel for damage, looseness, overheating, contamination, or fluid intrusion.

steps.title: Retest After External Causes Are Removed
steps.instructions: With questionable auxiliary devices disconnected, reconnect the Apollo to known-good AC power. Power the machine on, confirm the POWER FAIL alarm does not return, observe startup and standby operation, and verify normal AC power or battery charging indication.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed POWER FAIL alarm. Staff reported the machine was operating on battery, the alarm occurred during use, or the alarm appeared after an accessory was connected to an auxiliary outlet.
CCR.Cause: POWER FAIL alarm indicates AC power loss or possible short-circuit from a device connected to an auxiliary outlet. External power source, power cord, battery status, and auxiliary outlet devices were checked. Fault was isolated to a connected auxiliary device, wall power issue, or remained unresolved after external checks.
CCR.Resolution: Verified patient safety and removed machine from clinical use as needed. Checked AC outlet, power cord, battery status, pipeline supply, and auxiliary outlet accessories. Disconnected suspected auxiliary device and retested. Machine returned to service after alarm cleared and normal power operation was confirmed, or machine remained Out of Service for further repair due to persistent POWER FAIL alarm.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: POWER SPLY ERROR
description: Troubleshooting guide for the POWER SPLY ERROR alarm on a Drager Apollo anesthesia machine when an internal power supply fault is detected and operation may temporarily continue.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Notify anesthesia staff immediately if POWER SPLY ERROR occurs during active patient use. Confirm ventilation, gas delivery, vapor delivery, monitoring, and alarms are functioning. Prepare a backup anesthesia machine, manual ventilation option, or alternate patient support plan if the alarm persists or additional symptoms appear.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is POWER SPLY ERROR. Ask whether it appeared during startup, during case use, after movement, after being plugged in, intermittently, or continuously. Check for battery operation, charging issues, unexpected restart, display dimming, ventilator interruption, loss of gas delivery, additional alarms, odor, heat, or unusual electrical behavior.

steps.title: Check the External Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is connected to an approved hospital-grade outlet. Confirm the power cord is fully seated at the wall and machine. Check that the outlet is live using an approved test method, and try a known-good emergency power outlet if safe and allowed by facility policy.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord and plug for cuts, crushing, loose strain relief, bent blades, burn marks, fluid contamination, or intermittent connection when moved. Confirm the plug fits securely and the cord is not loose at the machine power entry point.

steps.title: Check Machine Position and Cable Strain
steps.instructions: Make sure the machine is not pulling tightly against the power cord. Check that the cord is not pinched under wheels, wrapped around equipment, or stretched behind the machine. Inspect the cord path carefully if the alarm appeared after the machine was moved.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Environmental or Physical Causes
steps.instructions: Inspect externally for fluid spill evidence, blocked vents, excessive dust, overheating, recent impact damage, burning smell, unusual fan noise, or visible damage near the power entry area. Remove the machine from service if unsafe damage, liquid intrusion, overheating, or electrical odor is present.

steps.title: Confirm Battery and Charging Behavior
steps.instructions: Check whether the machine indicates battery operation or charging. Confirm the battery symbol or battery status is normal. Ask staff whether the machine recently switched to battery unexpectedly or whether the alarm appeared after reconnecting to AC power.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: Do not power cycle during active patient use unless anesthesia staff direct it and patient support is protected. When no longer in clinical use, shut down normally, disconnect from AC briefly, reconnect to a known-good outlet, restart, and observe whether POWER SPLY ERROR returns.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: Review whether the outlet, power cord, cord strain, environmental conditions, external damage, and battery behavior have been ruled out. If POWER SPLY ERROR remains or returns, treat it as a likely internal power supply fault and remove the unit from service for evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed POWER SPLY ERROR. Staff reported the machine continued operating, but the alarm indicated a power supply fault.
CCR.Cause: Verified POWER SPLY ERROR condition. External AC outlet, power cord seating, cord condition, cable strain, battery status, and obvious external damage were checked. Alarm remained consistent with internal power supply fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed anesthesia machine from service, labeled Out of Service, and referred for repair/bench evaluation. DragerService support required for internal power supply fault.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: PRESSURE LIMITING
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Drager Apollo PRESSURE LIMITING alarm when the ventilator is operating with pressure limitation due to settings, tubing restriction, patient connection obstruction, or inspiratory microbial filter resistance.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the patient is connected to the Apollo. If ventilation is unstable, the alarm continues, or expected ventilation is not being delivered, notify anesthesia immediately. Have clinical staff use alternate ventilation if needed and remove the machine from patient use if the alarm cannot be corrected quickly and safely.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is PRESSURE LIMITING. Identify whether it occurs during mechanical ventilation, inspiration, after setting changes, or only with certain circuits or filters. Ask staff about low delivered tidal volume, high airway pressure, poor chest rise, or recent changes to circuit components.

steps.title: Check the Ventilation Settings
steps.instructions: Have anesthesia review the active ventilation mode and settings. Check pressure limit, peak pressure limit, inspiratory pressure, tidal volume, PEEP, and flow settings. Do not change clinical ventilation settings unless directed by qualified anesthesia staff.

steps.title: Inspect the Breathing Circuit and Patient Tubing
steps.instructions: Check hoses and tubing for kinks, crushing, occlusion, twisting, water accumulation, incorrect setup, stenosed tubing, or obstruction near the Y-piece. Straighten or replace affected tubing if needed.

steps.title: Check the Inspiratory-Side Microbial Filter
steps.instructions: Inspect the inspiratory microbial filter for soiling, moisture, secretions, high resistance, incorrect placement, or backwards installation if directional. Replace the filter if dirty, wet, clogged, or questionable.

steps.title: Check for Obstruction at the Patient Connection
steps.instructions: With clinical staff involved, verify the patient connection and airway interface are not restricted. Check for bite block issues, kinked endotracheal tube, stenosed tube, obstructed connector, HME/filter obstruction, secretions, moisture, or circuit compression near the patient.

steps.title: Check the Breathing System Setup
steps.instructions: Inspect the breathing system for correct assembly. Confirm inspiratory and expiratory hoses are connected correctly, no ports are capped incorrectly, accessories are installed in the correct location, and filters and adapters are appropriate for the circuit.

steps.title: Isolate Accessory-Related Causes
steps.instructions: If safe and allowed by local policy, replace questionable external accessories one at a time, including the breathing circuit, inspiratory microbial filter, HME/filter, patient connector, and flow-related adapters. Retest ventilation after each change using an appropriate clinical or test setup.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Machine Check After Corrections
steps.instructions: After correcting settings, tubing, filter, or setup issues, run the appropriate anesthesia machine checkout according to facility procedure. Verify no active PRESSURE LIMITING alarm, normal ventilator cycling, expected pressure behavior, appropriate delivered volume or pressure, no new alarms, and required leak/compliance check results.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo reported PRESSURE LIMITING alarm during ventilation. Staff reported ventilator was operating with pressure limitation and requested Clinical Engineering evaluation.
CCR.Cause: Inspiratory microbial filter was found soiled and restricted, causing increased inspiratory resistance and repeated PRESSURE LIMITING alarm.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from patient use for inspection. Checked ventilation settings, breathing hoses, patient tubing, breathing circuit setup, and inspiratory microbial filter. Replaced restricted microbial filter. Verified alarm cleared and machine passed required checkout before return to service.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Alarm: PRESSURE RELIEF
description: Troubleshooting guide for the PRESSURE RELIEF alarm on a Drager Apollo anesthesia machine when the internal pressure relief valve opens due to high system pressure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the anesthesia machine from patient use if pressure is not immediately controlled or if ventilation is affected. Have anesthesia staff ventilate the patient using an alternate method if needed. Do not continue using the machine if the PRESSURE RELIEF alarm repeats or system pressure remains elevated.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm shown is PRESSURE RELIEF. Ask staff when the alarm occurred and whether it happened during manual ventilation, mechanical ventilation, startup, fresh-gas adjustment, APL valve adjustment, or after connecting the breathing circuit or bag.

steps.title: Check the APL Valve Setting
steps.instructions: Check the APL valve setting. If the issue occurred during manual or bag ventilation, verify the APL valve was not set too high or fully closed. Have qualified staff adjust the APL valve to an appropriate setting for clinical use.

steps.title: Check Fresh-Gas Flow Settings
steps.instructions: Review oxygen, air, and nitrous oxide fresh-gas flow settings. Confirm flows are not excessive for the current ventilation setup. Have anesthesia staff adjust fresh-gas flow as clinically appropriate.

steps.title: Check the Breathing Circuit for Obstruction or Kinks
steps.instructions: Inspect the breathing circuit, patient tubing, Y-piece, filters, HME, bag hose, and expiratory path for kinked tubing, blocked filters, misconnected parts, occluded connections, collapsed hoses, fluid buildup, or foreign material.

steps.title: Check the Reservoir Bag and Bag Arm Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the breathing bag, bag connection, bag arm, and bag hose. Confirm the bag is not overfilled, blocked, twisted, or improperly attached.

steps.title: Check the Scavenging Setup
steps.instructions: Inspect the scavenging hose and waste gas path. Confirm the scavenging line is connected correctly and not kinked, crushed, blocked, or improperly restricted.

steps.title: Check for Accessory or Configuration Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether any accessory was recently changed. Check for a new breathing circuit, new filter or HME, new CO2 absorber canister, recent cleaning or reassembly, or recent vaporizer, circuit, or bag setup change.

steps.title: Run a Safe Functional Check
steps.instructions: After correcting any obvious issue, have qualified staff perform a safe checkout or leak/system test according to facility procedure. Confirm the alarm clears, ventilation pressure behaves normally, the APL valve responds normally, and the PRESSURE RELIEF alarm does not repeat.

CCR.Complaint: Drager Apollo anesthesia machine displayed PRESSURE RELIEF alarm during use. Staff reported internal pressure relief valve opened due to high system pressure.
CCR.Cause: APL valve setting and fresh-gas flow required verification. Breathing circuit, bag path, external accessories, and scavenging setup were checked for restriction or abnormal pressure buildup.
CCR.Resolution: Verified APL valve and fresh-gas settings, inspected breathing circuit and external gas paths, and confirmed alarm cleared with normal pressure behavior. Machine returned to service after functional check, or removed from service for further evaluation if alarm persisted.

title: Drager Resuscitaire Series Infant Warmer - Radiant Heater Failure
description: Troubleshooting absent or inadequate radiant heat output caused by power, mode, alarm, control, obstruction, or heater assembly faults.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
Remove the infant from the affected warmer and transfer care to an approved alternate warming device according to clinical protocol.

Do not test a suspected heater failure while an infant depends on the device for thermal support.

Expected outcome: The infant is safely supported on a working warmer before troubleshooting begins.

Why it matters: Failure of radiant heat can quickly compromise neonatal temperature management.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Reported Heater Failure
steps.instructions:
With the device out of clinical use, verify the reported condition:

Unit powers on but provides no apparent radiant warmth.
Heater output is selected but heat does not increase.
A System Fail, Power Fail, or other heater-related alarm is present.
Heating stops unexpectedly during operation.

The Resuscitaire warmer is designed with adjustable heater output from 0% to 100% in 10% increments and includes controller heating alarms such as System Fail and Power Fail.

Expected outcome: The complaint is reproduced or the reported alarm/behavior is documented.

If the heater operates normally and no alarm returns: Complete appropriate functional verification before returning the device to service, then stop.

steps.title: 3. Inspect for Visible Damage or Unsafe Conditions
steps.instructions:
Inspect the heater head, reflector area, control panel, column, power cord, and plug for:

Cracks, discoloration, scorch marks, or melted components.
Liquid intrusion or residue near the heater or controls.
Loose, damaged, or exposed wiring at externally visible points.
Unusual odor, smoke, or excessive heat from the housing.

Do not touch the radiant heater surface immediately after operation; allow adequate cooling time.

Expected outcome: No visible evidence of overheating, damage, or contamination is present.

If damage, smoke, burning odor, or abnormal heat is found: Stop troubleshooting, remove the unit from service, and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: 4. Verify AC Power and Power-Cord Connection
steps.instructions:
Confirm the power cord is firmly seated at the device and connected to a known-working hospital-grade outlet.

Check whether:

The display and indicator lights power normally.
A power-fail condition is present.
The outlet supplies power using an approved method.
The power cord has intermittent movement-related loss of power.

The Resuscitaire warmer requires AC power for heater operation; product specifications list 120 V or 220–240 V configurations depending on the model.

Expected outcome: The warmer receives stable AC power without a power-fail alarm.

If power restoration resolves the issue: Complete functional verification and stop.

steps.title: 5. Verify Heater Mode and Selected Output
steps.instructions:
Confirm the warmer is placed into the appropriate testable operating mode according to facility procedure and that heater output is intentionally set above 0%.

Observe whether:

The mode selection is accepted.
The heater output indication responds when adjusted.
The displayed output remains stable or drops unexpectedly.
An alarm occurs when heat is commanded.

Expected outcome: The control panel acknowledges a heat demand and displays an appropriate selected heater output.

If the heater was unintentionally set to 0% or an incorrect mode and normal heating returns after correction: Complete functional verification and stop.

steps.title: 6. Check for Alarm Conditions That May Interrupt Heating
steps.instructions:
Observe the alarm indicators and display while heat is commanded. Document any active or repeated indication, including:

System Fail.
Power Fail.
Check Patient alarm behavior.
High-temperature alarm.
Probe-related alarm when operating in a temperature-controlled mode.

Do not repeatedly silence or reset alarms simply to continue operation without identifying the cause.

Expected outcome: No unresolved alarm prevents normal heater operation.

If a persistent alarm occurs with loss of heating: Remove the device from service and proceed to repair evaluation.

steps.title: 7. Inspect the Heater Path and Physical Position
steps.instructions:
Verify that the heater head is properly positioned over the mattress area and that nothing blocks radiant heat delivery, including:

Linens, covers, accessories, or equipment placed between the heater and mattress.
Mispositioned heater head or bed components.
Heavy dust or residue visibly obstructing the reflector or heater area.

Do not disassemble or clean internal heater components during basic troubleshooting.

Expected outcome: The radiant heat path is unobstructed and the heater is correctly positioned.

If removing an obstruction restores normal warming: Complete functional verification and stop.

steps.title: 8. Assess Whether Radiant Heat Is Produced
steps.instructions:
With the unit empty and safely positioned for testing, command heater output and allow an appropriate observation period according to facility policy.

Observe from a safe distance whether:

Heat is produced over the mattress area.
The heater output indicator increases but no warmth is detected.
Heating is weak, uneven, intermittent, or accompanied by alarm activation.

The Resuscitaire uses a radiant heater and reflector arrangement intended to distribute heat across the mattress area.

Expected outcome: The warmer produces consistent radiant heat without abnormal alarms, odor, or visible damage.

If indicated output is present but radiant heat is absent or inadequate: Suspect a heater assembly, control, wiring, or internal power-delivery fault and stop further external troubleshooting.

steps.title: 9. Perform Final Functional Verification Only If the Problem Was Corrected
steps.instructions:
When an external issue has been identified and corrected, perform the required operational and safety verification for the warmer according to departmental procedure before returning it to use.

Verify that:

Power remains stable.
Heater output responds appropriately.
No related alarms remain active.
The device passes applicable safety and functional checks.

Expected outcome: The infant warmer is confirmed safe and functional for clinical use.

If verification fails: Remove the warmer from service and send it for repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Drager Resuscitaire powered on but did not provide radiant heat during setup for newborn care.
CCR.Cause: Verified stable AC power and selectable heater output, but the heater produced no detectable warming and the fault persisted with the unit empty.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the infant warmer from service, applied an Out of Service label, and routed the unit for bench evaluation of the radiant heater and internal heater-control system.

helpfulDetails: Heater output setting used during evaluation.
Alarm messages or indicator lights observed.
Whether warmth was absent, weak, uneven, or intermittent.
Visible discoloration, damage, unusual odor, or excessive heat.
Whether the heat path was obstructed.
Final device status and repair disposition.

title: Drager Resuscitaire Series Infant Warmer - Skin Temperature Probe or Servo-Control Fault
description: Troubleshooting missing, unstable, or inaccurate skin temperature readings and servo-mode operation affected by probe, connection, setup, or controller faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the warmer is supporting an infant, notify clinical staff immediately and move the patient to a known-working warmer or approved thermal-support method before troubleshooting. Do not troubleshoot an unresolved skin temperature or servo-control fault while the warmer is relied upon for patient temperature regulation. Expected outcome: The infant is safely supported on alternate equipment before the affected warmer is evaluated. Why it matters: In Servo Mode, the Resuscitaire adjusts heater output based on the connected skin temperature probe. An unreliable probe signal can interfere with temperature regulation.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Fault and Operating Mode
steps.instructions: Power on the warmer away from patient use and document the reported symptom: Probe alarm, no probe indication, or temperature reading absent; unstable, implausible, or rapidly changing skin temperature reading; Servo Mode unavailable or not maintaining expected heater response; System Fail or additional temperature-related alarm. Confirm whether the issue occurs in Servo Mode, since this mode depends on the skin temperature probe for automatic heater control. Expected outcome: The complaint is reproduced and clearly identified as a probe-detection, temperature-reading, or servo-control problem.

steps.title: Inspect the Skin Temperature Probe Externally
steps.instructions: Inspect the probe and its cable for: cut, cracked, stretched, pinched, or discolored insulation; bent, damaged, contaminated, or wet connector contacts; damaged probe sensor surface; evidence of improper cleaning, adhesive residue, or liquid intrusion; intermittent readings when the cable is gently positioned during bench testing. Do not use a visibly damaged probe for further clinical testing. Expected outcome: The probe is either found intact for continued evaluation or identified as damaged and removed from use.

steps.title: Verify the Probe Is Fully Connected
steps.instructions: Disconnect and firmly reconnect the skin temperature probe at the designated temperature-probe connection. Ensure the connector is correctly oriented and fully seated. Observe whether the probe alarm clears and whether a stable skin temperature value appears. Expected outcome: A loose or partially seated connection is corrected, and a stable temperature reading is restored. If resolved, document the correction and stop troubleshooting. Why it matters: The Resuscitaire identifies probe conditions including short circuit, open circuit, or no probe.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe Port and Nearby Connections
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the probe receptacle on the warmer for: bent or recessed contacts; cracks, looseness, or physical damage; residue, moisture, or contamination; evidence the connector does not latch or remain seated. Do not insert tools into the connector or attempt internal repairs during this evaluation. Expected outcome: The probe port appears clean and undamaged, or visible port damage supports removal from service for repair.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Compatible Skin Temperature Probe
steps.instructions: Using a clean, approved, known-good compatible skin temperature probe, connect it to the warmer and recheck the temperature display and Servo Mode behavior. Expected outcome: If the known-good probe operates normally, the original probe is defective and should be replaced. If the same fault remains with a known-good probe, the problem is likely within the warmer’s probe input or controller circuitry. Why it matters: This separates a removable accessory failure from a likely device-level fault without internal disassembly.

steps.title: Verify Displayed Temperature Is Reasonable and Stable
steps.instructions: With the known-good probe connected during bench evaluation, verify that the displayed skin temperature is present, stable, and reasonable for the test condition. The Resuscitaire skin temperature display range is specified as 18°C to 43°C, with accuracy specified as ±0.2°C from 31°C to 37°C. Expected outcome: A stable, plausible displayed temperature supports proper probe detection. Missing, erratic, or clearly implausible readings indicate the unit should not be returned to service.

steps.title: Check Servo Mode Response Without Patient Use
steps.instructions: Place the warmer in Servo Mode only during controlled bench evaluation, using an approved connected probe and following departmental testing practice. Observe whether: Servo Mode accepts the probe input; heater output responds appropriately to the sensed temperature condition; no probe-related, baby-temperature, high-temperature, or system-fail alarms remain active. The Resuscitaire Servo Mode uses a dual thermistor temperature probe and has a baby-temperature alarm condition of approximately ±1°C from the set point. Expected outcome: Servo Mode recognizes the probe and operates without unresolved alarms. If normal function is restored after replacing the probe, complete required verification before returning the device to service.

steps.title: Review Alarm Behavior and Final Device Status
steps.instructions: Document any alarms or indications observed during testing, including: Probe alarm; baby temperature alarm; high temperature alarm; System Fail indication; whether alarms cleared after probe replacement or reconnection; whether Servo Mode remained functional during bench evaluation. Expected outcome: The device either passes evaluation with a confirmed accessory correction or remains faulted and requires repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Labor and Delivery reported that the Drager Resuscitaire displayed a probe fault and would not regulate temperature properly in Servo Mode.
CCR.Cause: Skin temperature probe produced an intermittent reading, and the fault cleared when tested with a known-good compatible probe.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the defective skin temperature probe from use, replaced it with a functional probe, verified stable temperature display and Servo Mode operation, and returned the warmer to service.

helpfulDetails: Patient removed from affected warmer before troubleshooting
Operating mode when fault occurred: Pre-Warm, Manual, or Servo
Probe alarm, baby-temperature alarm, high-temperature alarm, or System Fail observed
Condition of probe cable and connector
Temperature reading behavior before and after probe replacement
Any visible damage, unusual heat, odor, or contamination
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Drager Resuscitaire Series Infant Warmer - Resuscitation Gas Flow or Pressure Issue
description: Troubleshooting absent, weak, unstable, or incorrect resuscitation gas flow or airway pressure before assuming an internal pneumatic failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the warmer is supporting an infant, immediately notify clinical staff and move resuscitation support to an approved alternate device or manual resuscitation method. Do not troubleshoot gas-delivery or airway-pressure concerns while the Resuscitaire is being relied upon for patient support. Expected outcome: The infant is safely supported using known-working equipment before troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: The Resuscitaire resuscitation module is intended to provide neonatal respiratory support; unreliable gas flow or pressure can directly affect ventilation and oxygen delivery.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Problem and Installed Configuration
steps.instructions: Determine exactly what was reported: No gas flow; Low or unstable flow; Inability to achieve expected airway pressure; Abnormal pressure gauge reading; Oxygen concentration concern on units equipped with a blender; Problem during T-piece or AutoBreath use, if equipped. Confirm which resuscitation options are installed, because available controls and gas requirements vary by configuration. The optional precision blender provides 21–100% oxygen, and AutoBreath is an optional resuscitation feature on equipped units. Expected outcome: The failure is clearly identified and matched to the device’s installed resuscitation configuration. If the reported issue cannot be reproduced: Document the report, complete an appropriate functional check, and return the unit only if performance is confirmed acceptable.

steps.title: Inspect the Device for Obvious External Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Remove the unit from use and inspect the accessible resuscitation components for: Damaged, cracked, pinched, or disconnected gas hoses; Loose wall-gas connectors; Bent fittings or damaged outlets; Contaminated, obstructed, or damaged patient gas outlet; Cracked pressure gauge lens or visibly abnormal gauge indication; Signs of impact damage to the resuscitation module; Unusual hissing, odor, heat, or evidence of a gas leak. Do not attempt internal pneumatic repair or adjustment during this inspection. Expected outcome: External physical damage, obstruction, or leakage is either identified or ruled out. If damage, leakage, or contamination is found: Stop troubleshooting, remove the unit from service, and send it for repair or bench evaluation.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Gas Sources Are Connected
steps.instructions: Confirm that the gas hoses are connected to the correct wall outlets and are fully seated. For units equipped with air/oxygen blending or functions requiring both gas sources, verify that both the oxygen and medical air connections are present and secure. A disconnected, crossed, or unavailable gas source can cause abnormal flow, pressure, or oxygen delivery. Expected outcome: Required gas supply connections are correct, secure, and available. If reconnecting a loose or incorrect hose resolves the issue: Perform a complete functional check before returning the warmer to service, then stop.

steps.title: Check Available Supply Pressure
steps.instructions: Verify the wall gas source using an approved method consistent with departmental policy, such as a verified test device or a known-working outlet comparison. For the Resuscitaire resuscitation module, the specified wall supply pressure is 40–75 psi (275–517 kPa). Cylinder-equipped configurations allow up to 2,900 psi maximum cylinder pressure. On units with visible supply gauges, compare the displayed indication with the expected connected gas source. Expected outcome: The external gas source is available and within the device’s specified supply-pressure range. If the supply pressure is absent or incorrect: Correct or escalate the medical gas source problem before condemning the infant warmer.

steps.title: Check Cylinder Supply, If Equipped
steps.instructions: If the unit is configured with reserve gas cylinders: Confirm the cylinder is the correct medical gas type; Confirm the cylinder is connected securely; Verify adequate remaining pressure on the appropriate gauge; Check for obvious regulator, hose, or fitting damage; Do not use a cylinder connection that leaks or cannot maintain a stable indication. Expected outcome: Reserve gas supply is present, correctly connected, and not the cause of the flow or pressure concern. If replacing an empty or improperly connected cylinder resolves the issue: Complete functional verification before returning the device to service, then stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Circuit and Disposable Accessories
steps.instructions: Disconnect the device from patient use and inspect the external breathing circuit, T-piece, mask interface, tubing, and any installed disposable components for: Kinks; Occlusions; Loose connections; Incorrect assembly; Damaged tubing; Fluid or debris contamination; Incorrect accessory selection. Substitute a known-good compatible circuit or accessory set when available. Expected outcome: External circuit or disposable accessory failure is identified or ruled out. If a replacement circuit restores normal flow and pressure: Complete the required operational check, document the defective accessory, and stop.

steps.title: Verify Flow Output Using Approved Test Equipment
steps.instructions: With the device off the patient and connected to proper gas sources, set up the resuscitation output for bench verification using approved testing equipment and facility procedure. Confirm that gas flow responds consistently to control adjustment. The documented patient gas supply flow control range is 0–15 LPM; the auxiliary flow circuit, when present, is also specified at 0–15 LPM. Expected outcome: Flow is present, stable, and responds appropriately through the expected range. If flow is absent, unstable, or cannot be verified despite correct external supply and accessories: Remove the unit from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Airway Pressure Performance Using a Test Lung or Appropriate Analyzer
steps.instructions: Using an approved test setup, verify that the unit can generate and maintain expected pressure without uncontrolled fluctuation or abnormal leakage. The Resuscitaire airway pressure limit is adjustable from 0–50 cm H₂O, with an internal preset limit listed as 60 cm H₂O. AutoBreath-equipped units list adjustable PEEP from 0–18 cm H₂O. Do not attempt internal regulator, valve, pressure-relief, or calibration adjustments as part of this troubleshooting guide. Expected outcome: Pressure output is stable and consistent with the selected test condition. If pressure cannot be established or is unstable: The device is not suitable for clinical use and requires repair or bench evaluation.

steps.title: Evaluate for an Internal Pneumatic or Control Failure
steps.instructions: If gas supply, wall outlets, cylinder supply, hoses, patient circuits, disposable accessories, and external test setup have all been verified, but the unit still has abnormal gas flow or pressure, the fault is likely internal to the resuscitation module or associated pneumatic controls. Examples may include an internal valve, regulator, gauge, blender, flow-control, or pressure-control problem. Expected outcome: External causes have been eliminated, and the unit is appropriately escalated rather than repeatedly tested in clinical service.

CCR.Complaint: Labor and Delivery reported that the Drager Resuscitaire produced weak and inconsistent gas flow during setup for neonatal resuscitation.
CCR.Cause: Medical air and oxygen wall supplies and patient circuit were verified, but resuscitation outlet flow remained unstable during bench testing with approved test equipment.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the Resuscitaire from service, labeled it Out of Service, and routed it for bench evaluation of the resuscitation module pneumatic controls.

helpfulDetails: Patient removed from affected device before troubleshooting
Resuscitation option installed, such as T-piece, blender, or AutoBreath
Oxygen and medical air connections verified
Wall supply pressure verified within specification
Patient circuit or disposable accessories replaced
Presence of hissing or suspected leak

title: Drager Resuscitaire Series Infant Warmer - Suction System Failure
description: Troubleshooting absent, weak, or inconsistent integrated suction caused by tubing, collection container, regulator, or supply-source issues.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the warmer is being used during newborn care and suction is unavailable or unreliable, notify clinical staff immediately and provide a functioning alternate suction source. Do not continue troubleshooting the suction system while it may be needed for active patient care. Expected outcome: The patient has reliable suction available before equipment troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Reported Suction Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm that the unit is equipped with the integrated resuscitation/suction module and identify the reported symptom: No suction; Weak suction; Suction cannot be adjusted; Vacuum gauge does not respond; Suction is intermittent; Collection container or tubing issue. The Resuscitaire suction circuit is specified as adjustable from 0 to 150 mmHg and uses a 40 ml disposable collection container on equipped configurations. Expected outcome: The exact suction-related failure is identified before parts are exchanged or the device is removed from service.

steps.title: 3. Inspect the Suction Patient Connection and Tubing
steps.instructions: With the device out of patient use, inspect the suction tubing and patient connection for: Kinks or collapsed tubing; Cracks, splits, or loose connections; Blockage from secretions or debris; Incorrect tubing installation; Missing disposable components. Replace questionable disposable tubing or suction accessories with compatible components, then retest. Expected outcome: Tubing is clear, secure, and properly connected. If normal suction returns, document the replaced disposable accessory and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 4. Inspect the Collection Container and Lid/Seal
steps.instructions: Check the disposable collection container for: Container full or contaminated; Container cracked or damaged; Lid improperly seated; Loose tubing connections at the container; Obstruction in the container inlet or outlet; Evidence that fluid has traveled beyond the container. Replace the disposable collection container when damaged, contaminated, full, or suspected of leaking. Expected outcome: The collection container is empty or replaced, correctly installed, and able to maintain a vacuum seal. If suction returns, document the cause and stop.

steps.title: 5. Verify Suction Adjustment and Gauge Response
steps.instructions: Using an appropriate test setup and not a patient, turn the suction control through its adjustment range while observing the vacuum gauge. Briefly occlude the test tubing tip to determine whether vacuum develops and whether the gauge responds smoothly. Expected outcome: Vacuum increases with adjustment and the gauge indicates suction. If the control does not change vacuum or the gauge remains at zero, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: 6. Check the Resuscitation Gas Supply Source
steps.instructions: Confirm that the connected gas source required for the resuscitation module is present, properly connected, and available at the warmer. Inspect external supply connections for: Disconnected wall gas hose; Closed or depleted cylinder, if equipped; Damaged hose or connector; Audible leak; Low or absent supply indication associated with the resuscitation module. Do not attempt internal pneumatic adjustment. Expected outcome: The external gas source is connected and available. If restoring the gas supply restores suction, document the external supply issue and stop.

steps.title: 7. Isolate the Failure to the Suction Path
steps.instructions: If available and approved for testing, install known-good compatible suction tubing and a new collection container, then repeat the occlusion test. If suction returns, the removed disposable component was the likely cause. If suction remains absent or weak despite known-good external components and an available gas supply, the failure is likely within the suction regulator, gauge, internal pneumatic path, or resuscitation module. Expected outcome: External disposable and supply-related causes are either corrected or ruled out.

steps.title: 8. Inspect for Contamination, Leakage, or Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the accessible suction module area for: Fluid contamination; Cracked fittings; Damaged adjustment knob; Loose external ports; Abnormal sounds indicating a leak; Unusual odor or evidence of prior liquid intrusion. Do not continue functional testing if contamination may have entered the device’s internal pneumatic components. Expected outcome: Any visible damage or contamination is identified and documented for repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Labor and Delivery reported that the Drager Resuscitaire integrated suction produced little to no vacuum during setup for newborn care.
CCR.Cause: Suction remained weak after replacement of the disposable collection container and tubing, with external gas supply present and no adequate vacuum response during occlusion testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the infant warmer from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for bench evaluation of the integrated suction/resuscitation module.

helpfulDetails: Reported symptom: no suction, weak suction, intermittent suction, or no gauge response
External gas source and connections verified
Vacuum gauge behavior during occlusion testing
Audible leaks, unusual sounds, or damaged fittings

title: Drager Resuscitaire Series Infant Warmer - Alarm, Display, or Power Fault
description: Troubleshooting loss of power, abnormal display operation, Power Fail/System Fail alarms, or alarm conditions caused by external connections or setup issues.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Resuscitaire is supporting an infant, notify clinical staff and move the infant to another approved warming/resuscitation device before troubleshooting. Do not continue using the warmer when power, display, or alarm operation is unreliable. Expected outcome: The infant is safely supported by alternate equipment before the affected warmer is evaluated. Why it matters: The Resuscitaire provides warming support and may include resuscitation components used during neonatal stabilization. A power, display, or alarm fault can prevent dependable monitoring or warming operation.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Power on the unit, if safe to do so, and observe the control panel for: No display or no response to power-on; Intermittent display operation; Power Fail indication; System Fail indication; Probe, Baby Temperature, High Temperature, or Check Patient alarm; Audible alarm without an obvious visual indication; Any reported error code or unusual display characters. Document whether the fault is constant, intermittent, or occurs only during startup or warming operation. Expected outcome: The actual alarm, display, or power symptom is identified before parts or accessories are suspected. If the reported condition cannot be reproduced: Perform normal operational verification according to department procedure before returning the device to service.

steps.title: 3. Inspect for Obvious Damage or Unsafe Conditions
steps.instructions: Disconnect the unit from patient use and inspect the warmer externally for: Damaged power cord or plug; Loose power inlet connection; Cracked, wet, or damaged control panel; Signs of fluid intrusion; Burnt smell, unusual heat, discoloration, or arcing evidence; Damaged heater head, column, or accessory attachments. Expected outcome: No external damage or hazardous condition is found. If damage, liquid intrusion, burning odor, or excessive heat is present: Stop troubleshooting, remove the device from service, and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: 4. Verify the AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the warmer is connected directly to an approved hospital-grade outlet. Test the outlet with an appropriate outlet tester or a known-working clinical device in accordance with facility practice. Do not rely only on the room’s wall indicator or assume the outlet is functional. Expected outcome: The outlet provides reliable AC power. If the outlet is defective or switched off: Move the warmer to a verified outlet and retest. If normal operation returns, document the external power-source issue and stop. Why it matters: The Resuscitaire identifies interrupted AC power as a Power Fail condition.

steps.title: 5. Inspect the Power Cord and Connection
steps.instructions: Verify that the power cord is fully seated, secured where applicable, and free from cuts, exposed conductors, loose blades, bent pins, or evidence of strain. Gently check whether movement of the cord or plug causes the display or power indication to flicker. Expected outcome: The unit remains powered consistently without intermittent operation. If a removable cord is damaged or suspect: Replace it with an approved known-good cord and retest. If power or display operation still cuts out with a verified outlet and known-good cord: Remove the device from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: 6. Check the Power Switch and Startup Behavior
steps.instructions: With the warmer connected to verified AC power, turn the unit on and observe whether: The display activates normally; Indicator lights illuminate appropriately; Audible alarms activate unexpectedly; The heater or mode indicators respond; The display blanks, resets, or freezes during startup. Expected outcome: The controller powers on and remains stable without an unresolved fault indication. If the unit does not power on, repeatedly resets, has a blank or unreadable display, or indicates System Fail: Do not continue clinical use. Remove it from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: 7. Differentiate a Power Fault From a Clinical Alarm Condition
steps.instructions: If the display is operating but an alarm is active, identify the alarm category before assuming the controller has failed. The Resuscitaire controller may indicate: Power Fail: AC power interruption; System Fail: system malfunction; Probe: shorted, open, or absent skin temperature probe; Baby Temperature: measured skin temperature outside the set-point tolerance; High Temperature: excessive measured skin temperature; Check Patient: required attention during Manual Mode operation. Expected outcome: A correctable accessory or operating-condition alarm is distinguished from a power or internal system fault. If a System Fail indication is present: Stop and escalate; do not attempt to clear it through repeated operation.

steps.title: 8. Check the Skin Temperature Probe and Connector, When Applicable
steps.instructions: If the alarm is related to Probe, Baby Temperature, or unexpected skin temperature display behavior: Confirm that the correct compatible temperature probe is connected. Inspect the probe connector, cable, and sensor end for damage, contamination, or loose connection. Disconnect and firmly reconnect the probe. Substitute a known-good compatible probe, when available. Expected outcome: A functional probe is recognized and temperature-related alarm behavior resolves when the accessory was the cause. If replacing or reseating the probe resolves the issue: Verify normal operation and document the defective or loose probe as the cause, then stop. If the fault remains with a known-good probe: Remove the warmer from service for further evaluation.

steps.title: 9. Check Whether the Alarm Is Related to Manual Mode Operation
steps.instructions: If staff report repeated alarm activation while the warmer otherwise powers and displays normally, confirm whether the unit was operating in Manual Mode. The Resuscitaire includes a Check Patient alarm function during Manual Mode operation; this may be reported as a device alarm even when the display and power system are operating normally. Expected outcome: The reported alarm is correctly identified as either expected mode-related behavior or an unresolved abnormal condition. If expected alarm behavior is confirmed: Educate the reporting department as appropriate, document the finding, and complete operational verification before return to service.

steps.title: 10. Evaluate Display Reliability During Basic External Testing
steps.instructions: Observe the display while performing safe, non-patient testing: Does the display remain readable? Do segments or indicator lights appear missing? Does the display flicker when the unit is moved or the cord is repositioned? Does the unit show inconsistent values or unexplained error indications? Can the alarm condition be matched to an external cause? Expected outcome: The display remains stable and provides reliable information. If the display is blank, incomplete, frozen, flickering, or unreliable after power and accessory checks: Remove the unit from service. A warmer with an unreliable display cannot be trusted for clinical use.

steps.title: 11. Perform Final Operational Verification
steps.instructions: After resolving an external issue such as outlet failure, damaged cord, loose connection, or defective probe, complete the department-approved operational and safety verification before returning the warmer to use. Confirm: Stable AC operation; Normal display function; Appropriate alarm indication and audible response; Proper recognition of connected accessories; No abnormal heat, smell, or intermittent behavior; Device status is clearly documented. Expected outcome: The warmer operates normally and passes required verification. If any alarm, display, or power concern remains unresolved: Do not return the unit to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Drager Resuscitaire displayed a Power Fail alarm and intermittently lost the control panel display during setup.
CCR.Cause: Found the warmer connected to a faulty AC receptacle that did not provide stable power; the warmer operated normally when connected to a verified outlet.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the original outlet from use through the appropriate facilities process, verified stable warmer power and display operation from a known-good outlet, completed operational checks, and returned the device to service.

helpfulDetails: Power cord condition and whether a known-good cord was substituted
Display behavior during startup and testing
Mode in use when the alarm occurred
Skin temperature probe condition and substitution result
Presence of unusual sound, heat, odor, or fluid exposure
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Drager Babytherm 8004 / 8010 Series Infant Warmer - Radiant Heater Not Warming
description: Troubleshooting absent or reduced radiant heat caused by power, heater enablement, operating mode, temperature sensor, alarm timeout, or heater-element failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the warmer is in use with an infant, immediately notify clinical staff and move the patient to an approved functioning warmer or other appropriate thermal-support device before troubleshooting. Do not attempt prolonged heater testing while a patient depends on the unit for warmth. Expected outcome: The infant is safely supported on working equipment before troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: A warmer that is not producing reliable heat can allow rapid neonatal heat loss.

steps.title: Inspect the Unit for Immediate Safety Concerns
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the radiant heater head, protective screen, control panel, power cord, plug, and frame for: Physical damage; Liquid contamination; Discoloration or signs of overheating; Burning odor; Loose or damaged power cord; Objects blocking or covering the radiant heater. Do not touch the radiant heater screen or heating surface during or immediately after operation. Expected outcome: No visible damage, obstruction, unusual odor, or unsafe condition is present. If damage, burning odor, or overheating is found: Remove the unit from service immediately and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Incoming Power and Controller Operation
steps.instructions: Confirm the warmer is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Verify the power cord is fully seated and inspect for signs of damage. Turn the controller on and observe the control panel: Green power indicator should illuminate during normal power operation. A red power-failure indication, continuous alarm, or blank/nonresponsive display may indicate an incoming power or device failure condition. The Babytherm operating instructions identify loss of power as a possible cause of alarm behavior and direct that the unit be checked for connection to power and removed from service when appropriate. Expected outcome: The unit powers on normally, the display is functional, and no unresolved power-related alarm remains. If the outlet, cord, or power-up behavior is abnormal: Correct the external power issue if possible. If the unit still does not operate normally, remove it from service.

steps.title: Confirm the Radiant Heater Is Turned On
steps.instructions: Verify that the radiant heater function is actually enabled at the control panel, not only the mattress heater or examination light. Confirm that the radiant heater on/off control has been activated and that the radiant heater output-level display is illuminated. For the Babytherm 8010, remember that the heated gel mattress is a separate warming function and does not confirm that the overhead radiant heater is operating. Expected outcome: The radiant heater is enabled and a heater output level is displayed. If the radiant heater was off: Turn it on, confirm the selected output level, and recheck for heat. If warming is restored, document the finding and return the device only after functional verification.

steps.title: Check for Active Alarms or Device-Failure Indications
steps.instructions: Observe the controller for: Red Inop. LED; Red or yellow alarm LED; Central alarm light; Audible alarm; Text display messages; Skin temperature sensor warnings; Heater-related warning behavior. A red Inop. indicator identifies a device fault and requires removal from service rather than continued use. Expected outcome: No unresolved device-failure indication or alarm is preventing dependable heater operation. If an Inop. condition or persistent fault message is present: Record the message exactly, remove the unit from service, and proceed to repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Operating Mode and Heater Output Setting
steps.instructions: With no patient on the device, place the radiant heater in manual mode for a controlled functional check. Confirm: The yellow man. indicator is illuminated. A heater output level is selected and confirmed. The output is not unintentionally set at a minimal level that may be difficult to perceive during a brief check. The Babytherm manual mode provides a selected heater output independent of skin temperature, while skin mode automatically adjusts heater output according to the connected skin temperature sensor and setpoint. Expected outcome: Manual mode activates normally and the selected radiant heater output remains displayed. If the unit was in skin mode with low output: Manual-mode testing may show the radiant heater is functional and the complaint was related to operating mode, setpoint, or sensor feedback. Document the finding and continue verification before returning to service.

steps.title: Check for Warmth From Both Radiant Heating Elements
steps.instructions: Allow a brief warm-up period in manual mode. Carefully place your hand at a safe distance below the radiant heater without touching the protective screen or heater assembly. Determine whether both infrared heating elements provide noticeable warmth. The Babytherm operating instructions specifically direct checking for warmth from both infrared heating elements at a safe distance beneath the radiant heater. Expected outcome: Both radiant heating elements produce warmth. If neither element warms: Continue to the alarm-timeout and control checks below, then remove from service if heat is still absent. If only one element appears to warm: Remove the unit from service for repair evaluation; radiant heat output may be incomplete or unreliable.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Heater Shut Off Following an Unacknowledged Temperature-Check Alarm
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff whether the heater initially warmed and later stopped. When operating in manual mode at heater levels 4 and above, the Babytherm generates a timed temperature-check alarm. If that alarm is not acknowledged, the heater switches off after the alarm period and remains off until the alarm is acknowledged. With no patient present and only as part of controlled equipment testing, review the displayed message and alarm state. If the display indicates the required temperature check alarm condition, acknowledge it and verify whether radiant heat resumes. Expected outcome: If heater shutdown was caused by the timed alarm, acknowledging the alarm restores heater operation. If heat returns after acknowledgment: The heater may be functioning normally. Document that the reported no-heat condition was associated with the unacknowledged timed alarm and verify normal operation before returning the device to service.

steps.title: Inspect Skin Temperature Sensor Connection When the Complaint Occurred in Skin Mode
steps.instructions: If the issue was reported while operating in skin mode, inspect the skin temperature sensor and connection: Confirm the sensor connector is fully seated in the correct socket. Inspect the sensor and cable for damage. Review the display for messages such as Plug in skin temp. sensor or Skin temp. sensor fault. Use a known-good compatible sensor if available and approved by facility practice. The Babytherm troubleshooting information identifies a disconnected or faulty skin temperature sensor as a cause of skin-mode alarms and directs checking the connection and replacing the sensor as appropriate. Expected outcome: The skin temperature sensor is intact, connected correctly, and does not generate an unresolved fault. If a known-good sensor corrects the issue: Complete functional verification and document the failed accessory replacement. If sensor faults continue: Remove the device from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Basic Function Only; Do Not Perform Internal Heater Repair at the Point of Use
steps.instructions: If power is present, the heater is enabled, manual mode is confirmed, alarm timeout is not the cause, sensor issues have been ruled out, and one or both radiant elements still do not heat properly, do not open the warmer or attempt internal repair in the care area. Expected outcome: External and readily verifiable causes have been ruled out. If the issue remains unresolved: Remove the device from service for qualified repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: NICU staff reported that the Drager Babytherm 8010 overhead radiant heater was turned on but was not producing noticeable warmth.
CCR.Cause: Verified normal incoming power and controller operation; radiant heater remained nonfunctional in manual mode with no warmth detected from either infrared heating element.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the infant warmer from service, applied an Out of Service label, and routed the unit for bench evaluation of the radiant heater system.

helpfulDetails: Manual or skin operating mode at time of failure
Alarm messages or Inop. indicator observed
Timed temperature-check alarm acknowledged or not applicable
Skin temperature sensor condition and whether a known-good sensor was tested
Whether one, both, or neither radiant element produced warmth
Any unusual odor, discoloration, heat, or physical damage
Final device status and repair routing

title: Drager Babytherm 8004 / 8010 Series Infant Warmer - Skin Temperature Sensor Fault
description: Troubleshooting a skin temperature sensor fault, disconnected probe, incorrect reading, alarm condition, or failure to operate safely in skin control mode.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the warmer is in use with an infant, notify clinical staff and move the patient to another approved warming device or safe thermal support method before troubleshooting. Do not rely on automatic skin temperature control while a sensor fault is active. A detached or failed sensor can produce unsafe temperature control conditions. Expected outcome: The infant is safely supported without dependence on the affected warmer before testing begins. Why it matters: In skin control mode, an incorrectly attached or disconnected sensor may measure air temperature rather than infant skin temperature, creating an overheating risk.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm Condition
steps.instructions: Power on the Babytherm only after it is removed from patient use. Confirm the reported condition, including any of the following: Red alarm LED blinking. Audible alarm sounding. Three middle segments or dashes blinking in the skin temperature display. Display message: “Skin temp. sensor fault” or “Plug in skin temp. sensor.” Expected outcome: The exact displayed message and alarm behavior are identified and documented. Why it matters: The operating instructions identify these messages as consistent with a faulty or disconnected skin temperature sensor.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Sensor Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the skin temperature sensor connection at the warmer. Confirm the central skin temperature sensor is fully seated in the yellow skin temperature sensor socket. Confirm the connector is not loose, partially inserted, contaminated, bent, cracked, or visibly damaged. Verify that the sensor cable is not accidentally connected to the peripheral temperature sensor location, when equipped. Reseat the sensor connector and observe whether the alarm clears. Expected outcome: A properly connected sensor produces a displayed skin temperature value without a sensor fault alarm. If the issue resolves: Complete an operational check, document the corrected loose or disconnected sensor, and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect the Sensor and Cable Externally
steps.instructions: Examine the entire visible sensor assembly from probe tip to connector. Look for: Cracked, cut, pinched, stretched, or frayed cable insulation. Fluid intrusion or contamination. Damaged probe surface. Bent, loose, or broken connector components. Evidence that the sensor was pulled or strained during use. Do not attempt to repair a damaged patient temperature sensor cable. Expected outcome: The sensor is either visibly intact or identified as damaged and unsuitable for further use. If damage is found: Remove the damaged sensor from use and replace it with an approved compatible sensor.

steps.title: Replace the Sensor With an Approved Compatible Sensor
steps.instructions: Disconnect the suspected faulty sensor and install a known-good, approved skin temperature sensor intended for use with the Babytherm 8004/8010. Use the correct sensor connection and confirm the replacement sensor is fully inserted. Expected outcome: The “Skin temp. sensor fault” or “Plug in skin temp. sensor” message clears and a plausible skin temperature reading appears. Why it matters: Drager documentation directs replacement of the skin temperature sensor for a sensor fault and specifies using approved sensors with the Babytherm 8004/8010.

steps.title: Verify Basic Sensor Response Off Patient
steps.instructions: With the replacement sensor connected and the warmer off patient, allow the sensor reading time to stabilize. Handle the sensor gently and observe whether the displayed skin temperature value responds gradually and remains stable without returning to a sensor fault alarm. Do not use this check to validate clinical temperature accuracy or return the warmer to patient care without required facility testing. Expected outcome: The displayed value is present, stable, and free of sensor fault alarms. If the alarm returns with a known-good sensor: Suspect a device-side connector, input circuitry, or internal measurement failure.

steps.title: Verify Skin Control Mode Can Be Selected Without Fault
steps.instructions: After the sensor alarm has cleared, select skin temperature control mode as appropriate for an off-patient operational check. Confirm: The skin control indicator activates. A skin temperature value remains displayed. No sensor fault alarm reappears. No abnormal alarm behavior occurs during the check. The Babytherm operating instructions indicate that, after replacing the sensor, the unit may be switched back from manual mode to skin control mode. Expected outcome: Skin temperature control mode is available without recurring sensor fault alarms. If the issue resolves: Complete required operational verification according to department policy before returning the warmer to service.

steps.title: Check for Use-Related Conditions Before Returning the Unit
steps.instructions: Before release, remind the requesting department that the sensor must be correctly positioned and secured during use. Verify that staff have an approved sensor and appropriate attachment supplies available. The sensor must not be placed underneath the infant, and secure attachment is necessary because a detached sensor can measure air temperature and increase overheating risk. Expected outcome: The equipment and approved accessories are ready for safe clinical setup.

CCR.Complaint: NICU reported that the Drager Babytherm 8010 displayed “Skin temp. sensor fault” with an audible alarm and would not maintain skin control monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Confirmed the alarm with the original skin temperature sensor connected; fault cleared after installing a known-good approved replacement sensor, identifying the original probe as defective.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the defective skin temperature sensor from use, installed a compatible replacement sensor, verified stable skin temperature display and normal skin control mode operation off patient, and returned the warmer to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact displayed message: “Skin temp. sensor fault” or “Plug in skin temp. sensor”
Red alarm LED and audible alarm behavior
Whether the original sensor was connected to the yellow socket
Whether the sensor connector was reseated
Replacement sensor used and whether it cleared the fault
Skin control mode operational check result
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Drager Babytherm 8004 / 8010 Series Infant Warmer - Heater Control or Overtemperature Alarm
description: Troubleshooting radiant heater control concerns, high skin-temperature alarms, or Babytherm 8010 mattress overtemperature alarms before escalating for repair.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If an infant is on the warmer when an overtemperature or heater-control concern occurs, notify clinical staff immediately and transfer the patient to a safe alternative warming environment as clinically appropriate. Do not troubleshoot heater operation or alarm behavior with an infant on the affected unit. Expected outcome: The infant is no longer exposed to a warmer with unresolved temperature-control concerns. Why it matters: A Babytherm alarm involving elevated temperature may indicate true excess heat delivery, improper sensing, or an external heat source that could injure the patient.

steps.title: Identify the Exact Alarm Message and Operating Condition
steps.instructions: Record the displayed message, alarm indicators, operating mode, and whether the unit is a Babytherm 8004 or 8010. Relevant messages may include: Skin temperature above 39 °C; Skin temp. deviation above X °C; Mattress temperature above 40 °C — Babytherm 8010 only; Mattress temp. deviation above 1 °C — Babytherm 8010 only; 15 min. patient temp check required during higher manual radiant-heater output levels. The 15-minute manual-mode reminder is not the same as an overtemperature failure. At manual heater levels 4 and above, the Babytherm provides a timed patient-temperature reminder alarm; if it is not acknowledged, radiant heating switches off. Expected outcome: The reported condition is accurately classified before testing continues.

steps.title: Inspect for Heat-Related Hazards or Obstructions
steps.instructions: With the unit removed from patient use, inspect the radiant warmer area and bed surface. Check for: Objects placed on or near the radiant warmer; Blocked ventilation openings; Signs of overheating, discoloration, unusual smell, or heat damage; Direct sunlight or another nearby radiant heat source; Phototherapy equipment contributing additional heat; A canopy being used while radiant heat is operating. Remove any external heat source or obstruction found. Do not continue testing if there is unusual heat, burning odor, visible damage, or evidence of overheating. Expected outcome: The warmer is free of external conditions that could create excessive heat or false high-temperature conditions. Why it matters: Drager warns that obstructing the radiant warmer can create a fire hazard, and additional heat sources such as phototherapy or sunlight may contribute to elevated skin-temperature alarms.

steps.title: Check the Skin Temperature Sensor and Connection
steps.instructions: If the alarm involves skin temperature or skin-temperature control: Inspect the approved skin temperature sensor for damage, contamination, loose adhesive, or cable strain. Verify the yellow central skin-temperature sensor connector is fully inserted into the correct socket. Confirm the sensor was positioned correctly during the reported event and was not detached, underneath the patient, or affected by external heat. Substitute a known-good compatible sensor, when available. A detached or improperly positioned sensor may give an inaccurate reading and can affect heater control when the unit is operating in skin-temperature control mode. Expected outcome: The skin-temperature reading is stable and the alarm no longer recurs with a verified sensor setup. Stop if resolved: If a damaged, disconnected, or improperly positioned sensor caused the issue, document the correction and complete appropriate operational verification before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Confirm the Selected Heater Operating Mode
steps.instructions: Verify whether the unit was operating in: Manual mode, where radiant heater output is set directly, or Skin-temperature control mode, where radiant heater output responds to the connected skin-temperature sensor. Confirm that the reported mode matches the intended clinical setup and that the warmer is not inadvertently operating at an inappropriate radiant heater level. For a reported Skin temperature above 39 °C alarm, verify that the condition was not caused by excessive external warming or an incorrect sensor setup. The Babytherm alarms when measured skin temperature exceeds 39 °C; in manual mode, the radiant warmer switches off during this condition. Expected outcome: Heater mode and displayed settings are appropriate, with no repeated high-temperature alarm during safe bench observation.

steps.title: Evaluate Radiant Heater Output Without Patient Use
steps.instructions: With the warmer empty and safe to test: Observe whether the radiant heater powers on normally. From a safe distance, confirm that heat output is present and appears consistent from the radiant heater. Monitor for unexpected continued heating, failure to produce warmth, repeated heater alarms, unusual smell, or abnormal display behavior. Do not touch the heater protective screen or upper radiant warmer surface. Expected outcome: The radiant heater responds normally without repeated overtemperature or heater-control alarms. Stop if abnormal: If heater output appears uncontrolled, absent, uneven, or accompanied by recurring alarms, remove the unit from service and escalate for bench evaluation.

steps.title: For Babytherm 8010 Only, Inspect Mattress Heater Conditions
steps.instructions: The Babytherm 8010 includes a heated gel mattress system; this step does not apply to the Babytherm 8004. Check that: The correct gel mattress is installed and properly positioned. The mattress is not damaged, excessively insulated, or covered with thick material that could affect heat transfer. The displayed mattress temperature and setpoint are reasonable. The unit is not reporting Mattress temp. deviation above 1 °C or Mattress temperature above 40 °C. If a Mattress temperature above 40 °C alarm is present, do not use the warmer for patient care. If radiant heat and mattress heat were being used together, verify that excessive combined heating was not contributing to the condition. Drager identifies a mattress temperature above 40 °C as an alarm condition for the Babytherm 8010. Expected outcome: Mattress heating remains stable without high-temperature or deviation alarms.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Perform a Safe Functional Observation
steps.instructions: After external causes, sensor setup, accessories, and settings have been checked: Turn the warmer off. Disconnect it from AC power briefly, following facility procedure. Reconnect to a known-good outlet and power the unit on. Observe startup, display messages, alarm indicators, radiant heater response, and, for the 8010, mattress heater behavior. Do not return the unit to patient use unless the reported issue is corrected and the unit passes applicable inspection and operational checks. Expected outcome: The warmer starts normally and operates without recurring heater-control or overtemperature alarms. Stop if resolved: Document the verified external cause and corrective action, then return the unit to service only after required safety and functional checks are complete.

CCR.Complaint: NICU reported the Drager Babytherm 8010 displayed a mattress temperature above 40 °C alarm during preparation for patient use.
CCR.Cause: Unit produced a recurring mattress overtemperature alarm during empty-bed testing after verifying the gel mattress placement, settings, sensor connections, and absence of additional heat sources.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the Babytherm 8010 from service, labeled it Out of Service, and routed it for bench evaluation of the mattress heater temperature-control system.

helpfulDetails: Whether the unit is Babytherm 8004 or 8010
Whether the issue involved radiant heat, skin-temperature control, or mattress heat
Selected operating mode and heater setting
Skin temperature sensor condition and whether a known-good sensor was tried
Use of phototherapy, canopy, sunlight exposure, or another nearby heat source
Mattress type and condition on Babytherm 8010
Indicator lights and audible alarm behavior
Any unusual heat, odor, discoloration, or physical damage
Whether the alarm cleared or recurred during safe bench observation

title: Drager Babytherm 8004 / 8010 Series Infant Warmer - Heated Mattress Failure on Equipped Units
description: Troubleshooting mattress heating alarms, poor warming, incorrect mattress setup, damaged gel mattresses, or suspected heated mattress failure on equipped Babytherm units.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If an infant is currently on a Babytherm with suspected mattress heating failure, overheating alarm, or mattress temperature sensor fault, notify clinical staff and transfer the infant to an appropriate working warming device before troubleshooting. Do not leave an infant on a gel mattress when mattress heating is not functioning or the device is switched off, because the gel mattress can cool the infant. Do not continue using a mattress system that may be overheating. Expected outcome: The infant is supported by a safe alternate warming method before equipment troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Confirm the Unit Is Equipped for Mattress Heating
steps.instructions: Verify the device configuration and control panel. A Babytherm 8010 equipped with mattress heating has a mattress temperature display and mattress temperature adjustment controls. If the device is a Babytherm 8004 without the heated mattress function, confirm that a foam mattress is installed. A gel mattress must not be used on the unheated 8004 configuration. Expected outcome: The reported problem is confirmed as a true heated mattress issue on an equipped unit, not an incorrect mattress/configuration mismatch.

steps.title: Inspect the Mattress Type and Placement
steps.instructions: Verify that the correct Drager gel mattress is present on a mattress-heater-equipped Babytherm 8010 and that it lies directly on the heated tray surface. Check that: A foam mattress has not been substituted on a heated 8010. Thick blankets, pads, or other insulating materials are not underneath the infant or between the gel mattress and heated surface. The mattress is not folded, kinked, punctured, brittle, cracked, or leaking. If the wrong mattress is present or the gel mattress is damaged, remove the device from clinical use until corrected. Expected outcome: The correct gel mattress is installed directly on the heated tray and is free of damage or obstruction that could interfere with safe heat transfer.

steps.title: Check Power and Initial Device Status
steps.instructions: Confirm that the power cord is securely connected to a hospital-grade receptacle, the receptacle provides power, and the unit switches on normally. Observe for: Power failure indication or continuous audible alarm. Red Inop. indicator. Blank, incomplete, or abnormal mattress temperature display. Any text message related to mattress temperature or device failure. If the red Inop. indicator is lit or the device reports a general device failure, remove the unit from service without continuing clinical use. Expected outcome: The unit powers normally without a general failure indication, allowing focused evaluation of the mattress heating function.

steps.title: Review the Displayed Mattress Alarm or Symptom
steps.instructions: Document the exact message and observed behavior. Common mattress-related conditions include: “Mattress temp. deviation above 1 °C” — measured temperature differs from the setpoint by more than ±1 °C. “Mattress temp. above 40 °C” — measured mattress temperature is too high. “Mattress temp. sensor fault” — temperature sensors in the heating panel are faulty. Temperature display showing dashes after extreme storage temperature exposure. If the unit displays “Mattress temp. sensor fault,” remove it from service immediately; this indicates a malfunction requiring repair evaluation. Expected outcome: The exact failure mode is identified and documented before determining whether a safe external correction is possible.

steps.title: Allow for Normal Warm-Up or Environmental Recovery
steps.instructions: If the device has recently been powered on, confirm whether the mattress is still warming toward the selected temperature. The manufacturer states that approximately one hour should be allowed for mattress warm-up before placing an infant in the bassinet. If the unit was stored in unusually cold or hot conditions and shows a low- or high-range temperature indication, allow it to stabilize in an appropriate room environment and reassess without a patient. If the mattress reaches and maintains the selected temperature without alarms, return the unit to service only after completing the required operational check. Expected outcome: A temporary warm-up or storage-temperature condition clears without persistent alarms or abnormal heating.

steps.title: Evaluate a Temperature Deviation Alarm Without a Patient
